assessments.summary: Remove the legacy templated-parent mechanism: delete all ENABLE_LEGACY_TEMPLATED_PARENT_SUPPORT-gated code (TemplatedParentScope, SetTemplatedParent2/UpdateTemplatedParentIfNeeded plumbing, _isLegacyTemplate), the public FrameworkTemplateBuilder delegate, and the legacy (owner, FrameworkTemplateBuilder) constructor overloads on FrameworkTemplate/ControlTemplate/DataTemplate/ItemsPanelTemplate, flipping the runtime fully onto the new NewFrameworkTemplateBuilder/TemplateMaterializationSettings path.
assessments.customerImpact: Mostly internal plumbing, but it removes genuine public API: the `public delegate View? FrameworkTemplateBuilder(object?)` and the legacy `(object? owner, FrameworkTemplateBuilder? factory)` constructor overloads on FrameworkTemplate/ControlTemplate/DataTemplate/ItemsPanelTemplate. Typical apps don't construct templates by hand (XAML codegen already emits the new TemplateMaterializationSettings overload), so most consumers are unaffected. Advanced users / third-party libraries that build templates programmatically with the old delegate signature, or call SetTemplatedParent2-style internals, would break and need to move to NewFrameworkTemplateBuilder. The bigger risk is behavioral: _isLegacyTemplate is still hardcoded true because flipping it to false currently breaks tests, so removing the legacy path is not yet safe at runtime without fixing those failures first.
assessments.decisionQuestion: The new templated-parent path is wired up and XAML codegen already emits it, but `_isLegacyTemplate` is hardcoded `true` because flipping it to `false` still fails tests (FrameworkTemplate.cs:37/94). Are we committing to fix those failing tests and fully switch off the legacy path in this major (vNext), and is removing the public FrameworkTemplateBuilder delegate + legacy template ctor overloads an acceptable public-API break, or do we keep them as obsolete shims for one more cycle?
assessments.notes: Issue #18672 is vague (a question: "is legacy templated parent intended to be removed in Uno 6?") and references the broader tracking issue #8339. Concrete state in repo: ENABLE_LEGACY_TEMPLATED_PARENT_SUPPORT is defined in ALL four Uno.UI target csprojs (Skia, Wasm, Reference, netcoremobile) — so this is NOT native-rendering-only; it lives in core cross-runtime XAML/DP code and affects Skia. 15 files reference the symbol. Key blocker: FrameworkTemplate.cs line 37 `private const bool _isLegacyTemplate = true; // Tests fails if set to false` and line 94 disabled `//_isLegacyTemplate = legacy;`. XamlObjectBuilder.cs:134-138 explicitly says the runtime XamlReader already uses the new impl and the [DynamicDependency] block "can be safely deleted once we moved to the new setup". Verified generated XAML (Out/.../*.cs) emits `Build_*(object __owner, TemplateMaterializationSettings __settings)` → the NEW NewFrameworkTemplateBuilder overload, so codegen is already migrated; only the runtime legacy fallback + public legacy delegate/ctors remain. Effort L because it requires fixing the test failures that currently force _isLegacyTemplate=true before the gated code can be deleted. Last meaningful commit 2026-05-12 (52fecbff4f) — actively maintained, not abandoned.

assessments.summary: Remove the legacy WASM DOM-interop object-marshalling API (public interface IJSObject and its companions JSObjectHandle, IJSObjectMetadata, JSObjectMetadataProvider, JSObject.Dispatch, plus the ManagedObject.ts JS side) from Uno.Foundation.Runtime.WebAssembly.
assessments.customerImpact: IJSObject is public API in the Uno.Foundation.Interop namespace, so removal is technically a source/binary break for any external WASM app that hand-rolled this object-to-JS-prototype marshalling. In practice this is extremely unlikely: it is a legacy WebAssembly-DOM interop path (the docstring says "DO NOT USE - generated using [GeneratedJSObject]", yet no such generator/attribute exists in the repo), it has zero consumers anywhere in this repo outside its own Interop folder, and it has been superseded by System.Runtime.InteropServices.JavaScript [JSImport]/[JSExport]. A typical app/library does not depend on it.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in code: IJSObject is declared public in src/Uno.Foundation.Runtime.WebAssembly/Interop/IJSObject.wasm.cs (namespace Uno.Foundation.Interop). It is consumed ONLY by its own companion types in the same Interop folder (JSObjectHandle.Create takes IJSObject; JSObjectMetadataProvider returns IJSObjectMetadata; JSObject.Dispatch is the [JSExport] entry point routed from ManagedObject.ts -> jsObjectHandle.Metadata.InvokeManaged). A whole-repo grep excluding that assembly folder returns ZERO hits for IJSObject / JSObjectHandle / IJSObjectMetadata / JSObjectMetadataProvider -> no internal consumers. The referenced [GeneratedJSObject] source generator does NOT exist in the repo (only the docstring mentions it). NOTE: the types are NOT currently marked [Obsolete] in this worktree (no Obsolete attribute in the Interop folder), so the PR 19230 deprecation is not reflected here as an attribute -- a deprecation cycle may already have shipped in a prior release, making removal-this-major the natural next step, but I could not verify the [Obsolete] ever existed from this worktree alone. This is WASM-DOM interop plumbing (builds JS prototypes via WebAssemblyRuntime.InvokeJS) tied to the native WASM DOM layer being dropped, but it lives in Uno.Foundation (a non-UI Foundation assembly) and is public surface, so I did NOT mark nativeOnly=true and judged it on normal merit. Danger 2: obscure public API customers almost never touch, dead in-repo. To remove coherently, delete all five .cs files plus the ManagedObject.ts JS counterpart (and any TS registration) together.

assessments.summary: Annotate the public static BaseActivity.Current property as nullable (BaseActivity?) on the Android activity host, reflecting that no current activity may exist.
assessments.customerImpact: Source-level breaking only for Android apps that read Uno.UI.BaseActivity.Current under #nullable enable and assign it to a non-nullable BaseActivity local/field without a null check (would get a CS8600/CS8601 nullable warning, or build error under TreatWarningsAsErrors). Runtime/ABI behavior is unchanged. Uncommon: BaseActivity.Current is an Uno-internal Android hosting detail most app code never references directly. Crucially, this targets the NATIVE Android UI host file (BaseActivity.Android.cs) which is part of the native rendering target being dropped this major version; the surviving Skia-on-Android BaseActivity already exposes Current as nullable, so there is no real-world gap.
assessments.notes: PR #18582 is CLOSED, not merged (mergedAt=null, closed 2025-09-15); it touched only src/Uno.UI/BaseActivity.Android.cs (+3/-1). In the current worktree that native file STILL has the non-nullable `public static BaseActivity Current => _current;` (line 86) and has no `#nullable enable`, so technically the original PR's edit is NOT in tree. However, the desired end-state already exists where it matters: the Skia-on-Android replacement src/Uno.UI.Runtime.Skia.Android/UI/Xaml/Controls/BaseActivity.cs line 82 already declares `public static BaseActivity? Current => _current;` with `_current` nullable. Because BaseActivity.Android.cs is the native Android UI AppCompatActivity host (native rendering target being dropped), the file itself disappears in this major version, so re-applying the closed PR's nullability tweak there is unnecessary — marked currentState=already-done on the surviving Skia target / nativeOnly for the dropped one. If maintainers keep BaseActivity.Android.cs around for any transition window, the one-line change (and dropping non-nullable assumptions on _current) is trivial (effort S). _current is set at line 265 (`_current = this;`) and starts null, so nullable is the correct annotation.

assessments.summary: Remove the public EventsBubblingInManagedCode dependency property (and EventsBubblingInManagedCodeProperty field, instance accessor, coerce callback) from UIElement, which controlled native-vs-managed routed-event bubbling and is meaningless once native bubbling is gone.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only apps/libraries that explicitly set UIElement.EventsBubblingInManagedCode (or reference EventsBubblingInManagedCodeProperty) to force certain routed events to bubble in managed code on the native targets (native Android Views, iOS/UIKit, WASM DOM) for native-interop scenarios. On Skia targets the property is already a no-op: PlatformSupportsNativeBubbling is false (UNO_HAS_MANAGED_POINTERS), every event is created with CanBubbleNatively=false, and IsBubblingInManagedCode short-circuits to managed bubbling without ever reading the property. A typical modern Skia-first app never touches it; usage is rare and advanced.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Once native bubbling is removed (CanBubbleNatively always false on all targets, since native rendering is dropped), should EventsBubblingInManagedCode be fully removed as public API, or kept as a deprecated/Obsolete no-op DP for a release to avoid breaking source-compat for apps that still reference it?
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: public DP defined at src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/UIElement.RoutedEvents.cs:200-220 (EventsBubblingInManagedCodeProperty + instance property + CoerceRoutedEventFlag). Only consumer is IsBubblingInManagedCode() (line 967-981), which reads the property ONLY when args.CanBubbleNatively is true; otherwise returns true (managed bubbling) immediately. CanBubbleNatively=true is set exclusively on native paths: UIElement.wasm.cs:665 (WASM DOM keyboard), TextBox.UIKit.cs:143, and native pointer args; on Skia PointerRoutedEventArgs.cs:22 sets PlatformSupportsNativeBubbling=false so it's always false and the property is dead. The DP is RoutedEventFlag-typed with Inherits|KeepCoercedWhenEquals metadata. Removing it should also drop/simplify the associated native-bubbling plumbing (CanBubbleNatively, IsBubblingInManagedCode, the [6]/[7] decision branches in RaiseEvent) and update the unit test Given_TappedRoutedEvent (which sets both EventsBubblingInManagedCode and CanBubbleNatively to simulate native bubbling) and the doc section in routed-events.md. Not auto-safe danger 1 because it removes genuine public API surface, but low impact (niche native-interop knob). No GitHub PR referenced; assessed purely from code. Effort is small for the DP itself but grows to M if the whole native-bubbling pathway is excised in the same change.

assessments.summary: Delete the WASM-only, no-op, EditorBrowsable-hidden FoundationFeatureConfiguration.Runtime.RethrowNativeExceptions property (and its now-empty Runtime nested class) which is already marked "Obsolete, remove in next major".
assessments.customerImpact: Effectively nobody. The property exists only under #if __WASM__ (native WASM/DOM target being dropped), is hidden from IntelliSense via [EditorBrowsable(Never)], is a pure no-op (getter always returns true, empty setter), has zero references anywhere in the repo, and is documented as deprecated. Any app that still references it (to control the long-gone JS-exception-rethrow behavior) would only fail to compile against the WASM-native flavor; the setting already does nothing, so no behavioral change. Removal source-breaks only the vanishingly rare app explicitly setting a deprecated, no-effect WASM flag.
assessments.notes: Located the single definition at src/Uno.Foundation/FoundationFeatureConfiguration.cs:33-47. The whole `public static class Runtime` block is wrapped in #if __WASM__ and contains only RethrowNativeExceptions; removing the property leaves the Runtime class empty, so drop the entire #if __WASM__ Runtime block. Code already carries the marker comment "// Obsolete, remove in next major" (line 42) and we are at the major version, so this is exactly the intended cleanup. Grepped the full repo: no consumers of either `RethrowNativeExceptions` or `FoundationFeatureConfiguration.Runtime` outside the definition. (Unrelated `RegisterAssemblyLoader` grep hits are a different symbol.) Classified native-only because it gates JS<->managed native-DOM exception interop under __WASM__, which is part of the native WASM target being dropped. Public API technically, but already hidden + deprecated + no-op, so danger 1.

assessments.summary: Remove the public read-only FeatureConfiguration.Timeline.DefaultsStartingValueFromAnimatedValue flag (hardcoded true) and inline its always-taken branch in Timeline animation From/To value computation.
assessments.customerImpact: Negligible. The property is a read-only (get-only) static bool hardcoded to true with no setter, so no app can toggle behavior through it; the only conceivable break is a source/binary compile error for code that *reads* the symbol, which is vanishingly rare. Its sole consumer is internal animation code (Timeline.animation.cs GetDefaultTargetValue); inlining the always-true branch (GetValueCore) preserves current runtime behavior exactly, leaving the GetNonAnimatedValue() branch unreachable from here.
assessments.notes: Located both sites concretely. FeatureConfiguration is `public static class` (Uno.UI namespace), nested `Timeline` is `public static class`, and the property is `public static bool DefaultsStartingValueFromAnimatedValue { get; } = true;` (FeatureConfiguration.cs:958) — public surface but READ-ONLY (no setter), so it is a one-way always-on flag, not a real toggle. Only one usage in the codebase: Timeline.animation.cs:440 in GetDefaultTargetValue(), `flag ? GetValueCore() : GetNonAnimatedValue()`. Since flag is always true, the else branch is dead. Removing the property requires inlining to always use GetValueCore(); GetNonAnimatedValue() remains used elsewhere (still referenced via _owner.GetNonAnimatedValue) so it is not orphaned. This is cross-platform Uno.UI animation code, NOT native-only rendering. Danger nudged from 1 to 2 only because it technically removes a public (albeit read-only/always-on) symbol rather than a truly internal flag; practical customer impact is essentially zero.

assessments.summary: Remove the redundant legacy "bootstrapper" meta-NuGet packages (Uno.WinUI.Skia.X11/MacOS/Linux.FrameBuffer and Uno.WinUI.WebAssembly) that ship no assets and only redirect to the corresponding Uno.WinUI.Runtime.Skia.* / Runtime.WebAssembly packages, along with their pack/diff plumbing in build/Uno.UI.Build.csproj and template PackageReferences.
assessments.customerImpact: Apps with an explicit PackageReference to a removed meta-package (e.g. Uno.WinUI.Skia.X11 or Uno.WinUI.WebAssembly) get a NuGet restore failure until they switch to the corresponding Uno.WinUI.Runtime.Skia.* / Uno.WinUI.Runtime.WebAssembly package. Current Uno templates and most up-to-date apps already reference the Runtime.* packages directly, so few maintained projects depend on these; mainly older/hand-rolled head projects are affected. Pure packaging breakage, easy to migrate (one-line reference swap), no API/source change.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should the remaining redundant meta-packages (Uno.WinUI.Skia.X11/MacOS/Linux.FrameBuffer + Uno.WinUI.WebAssembly) be removed entirely, or kept as thin compat shims for one more release to avoid breaking older apps that reference them directly?
assessments.notes: PR #17788 is CLOSED (never merged); linked issue #17686 ("Cleanup redundant NuGet packages") is still OPEN, so the work was deferred, not completed. Confirmed in-repo: the cleanup is PARTIALLY done — build/nuget/Uno.WinUI.Skia.Gtk.nuspec and Uno.WinUI.Skia.Wpf.nuspec are already gone (Gtk/Wpf heads were dropped separately), but 4 redundant nuspecs remain: Skia.X11, Skia.MacOS, Skia.Linux.FrameBuffer, WebAssembly. build/Uno.UI.Build.csproj lines 157-160 (_NuspecFiles), 224-228 (pack Exec), and 259-262 (_diffPackage) still reference them. Each nuspec is a pure meta-package: empty lib (lib\netstandard1.0\_._) plus a uno.png, dependencies pointing at Uno.WinUI + the real Uno.WinUI.Runtime.Skia.* / Runtime.WebAssembly package. In-repo consumers still referencing the meta IDs: src/SolutionTemplate/UnoAppWinUI/*.Wasm and *.Skia.Linux.FrameBuffer, and UnoAppWinUILinuxValidation/*.Wasm + *.Skia.Linux.FrameBuffer (PackageReference Include="Uno.WinUI.WebAssembly"/"Uno.WinUI.Skia.Linux.FrameBuffer"). These template references must be repointed to the Runtime.* packages as part of the removal. This is a build/packaging change, not native rendering source; nativeOnly=false because it touches NuGet packaging (incl. the WASM meta-package) rather than native-target rendering code being deleted. Danger 2: external-facing packaging surface but obscure/legacy and trivially migratable. needsDecision=true because whether to hard-remove vs keep deprecated compat shims for a release is a maintainer call.

assessments.summary: Change the public NavigatingCancelEventArgs(NavigationMode, NavigationTransitionInfo, object, Type) constructor to internal so the sealed event-args type matches WinUI (which exposes no public constructor).
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only code that directly calls `new NavigatingCancelEventArgs(...)`. In WinUI this type is produced solely by the framework and delivered through the Frame/Page `Navigating` event, so apps read it but virtually never construct it. The only realistic external callers are unit tests that synthesize the args. All in-repo call sites are internal to Uno.UI (Frame.legacy.cs, NavigationHelpers.cs, and the mux paths), so making the ctor internal keeps the framework compiling. Low likelihood a typical app or library depends on the public ctor.
assessments.notes: Confirmed the ctor is still `public` at src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Navigation/NavigatingCancelEventArgs.cs:13 (sealed partial class). WinUI/UWP's NavigatingCancelEventArgs is a runtime-class event-args type with no public constructor; the public ctor is an Uno-only divergence. All four construction sites are inside the Uno.UI assembly (Frame.legacy.cs:188 directly; NavigationHelpers.cs:60 via CreateINavigatingCancelEventArgs, called from Frame.partial.mux.cs:701 and Page.mux.cs:166), so changing it to `internal` compiles without touching callers. The Generated stub (NavigatingCancelEventArgs.cs under Generated/) declares the type but no ctor, so no conflict there. Fix is a one-keyword change (public -> internal). Not a rendering API, so nativeOnly=false even though native targets are being dropped.

assessments.summary: Narrow the Uno-only `protected virtual ComboBox.OnIsDropDownOpenChanged(bool, bool)` extensibility hook to `private protected` (or `private`), removing it from the public/derivable surface.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only an app/library that subclasses ComboBox AND overrides this specific Uno-invented `OnIsDropDownOpenChanged(bool,bool)` method. It is NOT a WinUI API (WinUI's equivalent is a private C++ method), so it doesn't exist on Windows/WinAppSDK — any code overriding it is already Uno-only and non-portable. Realistically near-zero apps do this; the intended override points are IsDropDownOpened/IsDropDownClosed events and the native partial methods (OnIsDropDownOpenChangedPartial/...Native), not this protected wrapper.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should the Uno-only `OnIsDropDownOpenChanged(bool,bool)` be `private protected` (keep it overridable by Uno's own ComboBox-derived types if any future native partials need it) or just plain `private` (it's only called from OnPropertyChanged2 and only fans out to the native partial methods, so `private` is sufficient unless a derived control must intercept it)?
assessments.notes: Two methods share the name: (1) the proposed target `protected virtual OnIsDropDownOpenChanged(bool,bool)` at ComboBox.custom.cs:219, a Uno-invented hook that only calls partial methods OnIsDropDownOpenChangedPartial/...Native (implemented in native .Android.cs/.iOS.cs); (2) `private void OnIsDropDownOpenChanged()` at ComboBox.partial.mux.cs:698, the actual ported WinUI logic — already private, not affected. Both are invoked from OnPropertyChanged2 (ComboBox.partial.mux.cs:626-627). Confirmed via grep: no `override` of OnIsDropDownOpenChanged anywhere in the repo; the only ComboBox subclass found is the test helper PopupPlacementComboBox, which does not override it. WinUI reference (ComboBox_Partial.cpp tag winui3/release/1.6.1) has this as a private method, so reducing visibility moves Uno closer to WinUI parity. Since the partial methods are the real native extension seam, plain `private` is likely safe; `private protected` is the conservative choice. Marked verify -> needs maintainer decision.

assessments.summary: Proposal to change ItemsControl.OnItemsChanged's parameter from object to IVectorChangedEventArgs and/or tighten its visibility from protected to private protected; investigation shows the current signature already matches WinUI exactly, so the change would break parity rather than fix anything.
assessments.customerImpact: If applied: subclasses of ItemsControl/ListView/ComboBox/FlipView overriding OnItemsChanged(object e) break compilation; uncommon but valid extensibility. The proposal itself is mis-specified relative to WinUI.
assessments.decisionQuestion: WinAppSDK's IDL declares ItemsControl.OnItemsChanged as 'overridable void OnItemsChanged(Object e)' (protected virtual, object param), which Uno already matches exactly. Do we still want to deliberately diverge by narrowing the parameter to IVectorChangedEventArgs and/or making it private protected — or should this item be dropped to preserve WinUI parity?
assessments.notes: Located at src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Controls/ItemsControl/ItemsControl.cs:254 — `protected virtual void OnItemsChanged(object e)`. The internal caller OnItemsVectorChanged (line 90-93) does pass an IVectorChangedEventArgs into it, which is presumably why the BC author thought the parameter "should" be IVectorChangedEventArgs. However the authoritative WinAppSDK IDL (D:\Work\microsoft-ui-xaml2\dxaml\xcp\dxaml\idl\winrt\controls\microsoft.ui.xaml.controls.controls.idl:1875) declares it as `overridable void OnItemsChanged(Object e)` — i.e. protected-virtual with an Object parameter — so Uno is already 1:1 with WinUI. Both proposed mutations (IVectorChangedEventArgs param; private protected visibility) would BREAK parity. Override sites that would need touching if pursued: ComboBox (mux), FlipView (managed/reference/iOSAndroid), TabViewListView (mux), plus unit/runtime test subclasses. Danger 3 reflects that it touches a protected, overridable member on a heavily-subclassed base control; nativeOnly=false (this is the cross-runtime/Skia control, not native-only rendering code). Generated stub note: src/Uno.UI/Generated/.../ItemsControl.cs:109 'Skipping already declared method ...OnItemsChanged(object)'. Recommendation: drop BC10 — code already in desired (WinUI-matching) state.

assessments.summary: Make the public setter of PageStackEntry.SourcePageType non-public (internal/private set) to match WinUI's get-only property, keeping internal Uno usages working.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only apps/libraries that reach into a Frame back/forward-stack PageStackEntry and reassign its SourcePageType after construction (e.g. entry.SourcePageType = typeof(X)). This pattern doesn't exist in WinUI (the property is get-only there), so portable code never relies on it; the page type is normally supplied via the PageStackEntry constructor or Frame.Navigate. Very unlikely a typical app depends on it. All internal Uno setters (PageStackEntry.Create, Frame.legacy.cs SetSourcePageType helper, Frame.HotReload.cs) are same-assembly and keep working with an internal set.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: PageStackEntry.Properties.cs line 38 currently has `set => SetValue(SourcePageTypeProperty, value);` as a PUBLIC setter on the public Type SourcePageType property (namespace Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Navigation, Uno.UI assembly). WinUI/UWP exposes SourcePageType as get-only, so this is a Uno divergence; the change reduces the setter visibility (internal set, or private set with the existing internal call sites kept). Internal setter call sites: PageStackEntry.cs:68 (Create factory), Frame.legacy.cs:73-75 (SetSourcePageType local fn), Frame.HotReload.cs:68 (gated #if HAS_UNO). All three are in the Uno.UI assembly, so `internal set` is the cleanest fix and needs no other edits. Note: the many `.SourcePageType =` matches in NavigationHistory.mux.cs and Given_Frame.cs unit/runtime tests are on Frame.SourcePageType (a different property) and are NOT affected. Low danger (rubric 2): visibility reduction on an obscure public-surface setter that customers almost never touch and that diverges from WinUI. The existing IL2067/IL2073 suppression comments around the setter would remain valid; SetSourcePageType helper in Frame.legacy.cs could even be inlined once the setter is internal.

assessments.summary: Change Frame.BackStack's property setter from public to internal (get-only public surface), matching WinUI's get-only IList<PageStackEntry> BackStack contract.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only apps that assign a whole new IList to Frame.BackStack (e.g. `frame.BackStack = myList;`). This is rare and non-portable: WinUI/UWP exposes BackStack as get-only, so such code never compiled against WinUI and isn't something a typical app or library does. The common pattern (frame.BackStack.Add/.Remove/.Clear, or reading the list) is unaffected because those operate on the returned IList, not the setter. Code that relied on wholesale replacement (uncommon, mostly seen when restoring navigation state) would need to mutate the existing collection instead.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: Frame.Properties.cs:17-21 declares `public IList<PageStackEntry> BackStack { get => ...; set => SetValue(BackStackProperty, value); // TODO: Setter should not be public }`. The TODO comment is an explicit author note that this exact change is intended, so the item is not yet done. The fix is a one-liner: change `set =>` to `internal set =>` (BackStackDepth already uses this `internal set` pattern at line 39, so it's the established convention in the same file). Internal call sites are safe: the only two setter usages are internal initialization (Frame.legacy.cs:46 `BackStack = backStack;` and Frame.partial.mux.cs:41 `BackStack = GetBackStack();`) — both within the same assembly, so an internal setter keeps them compiling. All other BackStack references (Add/Remove/Clear/.Count/.Any/foreach) operate on the returned list and are unaffected. The BackStackProperty DependencyProperty itself stays public, only the CLR setter visibility is reduced. Diverges from WinUI which defines BackStack as get-only. Code review only (no compile run); change is mechanical and low risk.

assessments.summary: Change the type of Microsoft.UI.Xaml.WindowActivatedEventArgs.WindowActivationState from Windows.UI.Core.CoreWindowActivationState to the correct WinUI type Microsoft.UI.Xaml.WindowActivationState.
assessments.customerImpact: Source-breaking for any app whose Window.Activated handler reads e.WindowActivationState and compares it to Windows.UI.Core.CoreWindowActivationState.* — they must switch to Microsoft.UI.Xaml.WindowActivationState.* (mechanical, same member names/values CodeActivated/Deactivated/PointerActivated). Window.Activated is a moderately common app hook (splash/pause/active UI, focus tracking) on Skia desktop, so a non-trivial minority of apps touch it; most apps that only subscribe but never inspect the state are unaffected. The UWP-flavor (Windows.UI.Core.WindowActivatedEventArgs) is unchanged and stays on CoreWindowActivationState.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Window/WindowActivatedEventArgs.cs line 26 declares `public CoreWindowActivationState WindowActivationState { get; }` (Windows.UI.Core), but per WinUI/WinAppSDK 2.0 (verified via Microsoft Learn) the Microsoft.UI.Xaml flavor must return Microsoft.UI.Xaml.WindowActivationState. The correct enum already exists as a generated stub (Generated/3.0.0.0/Microsoft.UI.Xaml/WindowActivationState.cs) with identical members/values, so the fix is: re-type the property + constructor to Microsoft.UI.Xaml.WindowActivationState (mapping the internal CoreWindowActivationState used by the windowing pipeline at the boundary), update internal consumers (WindowChrome.cs:95, MacOSWindowHost.cs:236, BaseWindowImplementation, Window.alc.cs), and remove the suppression at SymbolMatchingHelpers.cs:190-194. That suppression carries `// TODO: Match API with WinUI.`, confirming the divergence was deliberately deferred and is exactly what this BC fixes. Note: the UWP flavor Windows.UI.Core.WindowActivatedEventArgs correctly stays on CoreWindowActivationState — do NOT touch it. Design is unambiguous (WinUI dictates the target type); no decision required. Not native-only: this is cross-platform Microsoft.UI.Xaml public surface consumed on Skia desktop.

assessments.dependencies: No dependency on other items; this is a standalone base-class realignment.
assessments.notes: Verified WinUI hierarchy from local MUX IDL: UserControl : Control with UIElement Content + ContentProperty (controls.idl:2255), Page : UserControl (controls2.idl:1947). Uno today: UserControl.cs line 7 'public partial class UserControl : ContentControl', Page : UserControl. The divergence is the extra ContentControl Uno inserts; the fix still applies (not done). All UserControl Content rendering currently comes from ContentControl (ContentPresenter/OnContentChanged/template -- 30 refs in ContentControl.cs), so re-parenting requires reimplementing content hosting the WinUI way (direct single UIElement child, no template). Danger 5: pervasive base-class change touching every Page and UserControl. NOT native-only -- core cross-platform Uno.UI layer shipping on Skia; risk is independent of native-target removal. Recommend major-version-gated, decision-required, with broad runtime-test coverage (Page navigation, ContentPresenter-less rendering, XAML content assignment).
assessments.summary: Change Uno's UserControl to inherit directly from Control (with its own UIElement-typed Content/ContentProperty) instead of from ContentControl, matching the WinUI hierarchy Control -> UserControl -> Page.
assessments.customerImpact: Affects essentially every Uno app and control library: in WinUI UserControl : Control, but Uno inserts an extra ContentControl (Control -> ContentControl -> UserControl -> Page). Dropping ContentControl removes ContentTemplate/ContentTemplateSelector/ContentTransitions and the object-typed Content setter from every UserControl and Page (and every app's custom subclass). `x is ContentControl` checks and casts against a UserControl or Page flip from true to false; XAML/code that set ContentTemplate on a UserControl, or relied on ContentControl-typed Content, break. WinUI's UserControl.Content is typed UIElement (not object), a further source/semantic difference. Many apps subclass UserControl/Page, so breakage is broad even though most don't touch the ContentControl extras directly.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Do we re-parent UserControl onto Control to match WinUI (reimplementing single-UIElement Content hosting without ContentControl's ContentPresenter/template machinery, and accepting that ContentTemplate/ContentTemplateSelector/ContentTransitions vanish from UserControl/Page and is-ContentControl checks flip), or keep the extra ContentControl in the chain for backward compatibility?

assessments.summary: Add the `sealed` modifier to the public `Microsoft.UI.Xaml.PropertyPath` class so it cannot be subclassed, matching WinUI (which the VS designer expects).
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only consumers that declare a type deriving from Microsoft.UI.Xaml.PropertyPath. PropertyPath is a trivial string-wrapper used in bindings/setters/animations and is constructed from a path string (with an implicit string conversion); subclassing it has no practical purpose and is impossible in real WinUI (where it is already sealed). No derived types exist anywhere in this repo, and a typical app/library never subclasses it, so real-world breakage is near-zero. The change is purely an extensibility reduction (sealing), not a signature change, so existing callers that construct/consume PropertyPath are unaffected.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: the public type is `public partial class PropertyPath` in src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Data/PropertyPath.cs (NOT sealed) — so the change still applies. Fix is a one-liner: add `sealed` (e.g. `public sealed partial class PropertyPath`). WinUI's Microsoft.UI.Xaml.PropertyPath is sealed, so this aligns Uno with the framework and the VS designer's expectation. Grepped all *.cs under src for `: PropertyPath` and found ZERO derived types; the 151 references across 40 files all construct/consume it. Two side-notes (out of scope for this item but worth flagging): (1) the hand-written public class does NOT inherit DependencyObject, whereas the Generated stub declares `PropertyPath : global::Microsoft.UI.Xaml.DependencyObject` — pre-existing divergence from WinUI, unrelated to sealing. (2) The partial spans only these two files; placing `sealed` on either partial declaration suffices, but to avoid a CS0263 mismatch the `sealed` should be applied consistently (the generated stub is auto-generated and must not be hand-edited, so add `sealed` to the hand-written declaration and confirm the generator emits it too, or that mixing is accepted by the compiler). Not native-rendering code — it's a cross-platform binding/data type in Uno.UI. No GitHub reference to verify against; assessment is from the code.

assessments.summary: Re-apply the reverted change so the internal virtual OnPropertyChanged2 (and IDependencyObjectInternal / DependencyObjectGenerator) take a lightweight readonly struct PropertyChangedParams instead of DependencyPropertyChangedEventArgs, enabling the #if false'd PropertyChangedParams.cs struct.
assessments.customerImpact: OnPropertyChanged2 is an internal virtual method (IDependencyObjectInternal is internal); it is NOT public API, so typical apps cannot see or call it. The real break is binary/version-skew: friend assemblies that override it via InternalsVisibleTo -- notably Uno.Toolkit -- get MissingMethodException at runtime when a Toolkit built against the old signature runs on the new Uno.UI (the exact reason #17414 reverted it). Impact is limited to first-party/partner libraries that ship cross-version; a normal customer app rebuilt against the new Uno is unaffected. Deferred to a major version on purpose (the struct sits under '#if false // To be brought back in Uno 6'), so it is appropriate to land here.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Is re-introducing the readonly-struct PropertyChangedParams still the desired design, given that since the #17414 revert DependencyObjectStore was rewritten to use a pooled/reused mutable DependencyPropertyChangedEventArgs (_dpChangedEventArgsPool.Rent/Return) -- which may already capture the allocation win the struct was meant to provide -- and given that DependencyPropertyChangedEventArgs additionally carries precedence/bypass fields that PropertyChangedParams drops?
assessments.notes: Confirmed the repo is in the REVERTED state: PropertyChangedParams.cs is wrapped in '#if false // To be brought back in Uno 6'; the generator (DependencyObjectGenerator.cs lines 796/800), IDependencyObjectInternal.cs line 12, and all ~30 control overrides currently use DependencyPropertyChangedEventArgs; DependencyObjectStore.cs line 1851 has the struct construction commented out. So re-applying BC16 still applies. Re-application = enable the struct + flip the generator/interface/all overrides + reconstruct it at the call site. The PR diff (#17414) showed a 'new DependencyPropertyChangedEventArgs(...)' call site, but current code at DependencyObjectStore.cs ~1912-1955 uses a rented/pooled mutable eventArgs and passes it to doInternal.OnPropertyChanged2 -- so a faithful re-apply must reconcile against the newer pooling code, not the old diff. Danger kept at 2 (internal-only surface; only friend-assembly/version-skew consumers like Uno.Toolkit break, exactly what #17414 cites). Not native-only -- touches cross-platform core DP plumbing. Skia targets compile this same generic Uno.UI code, so it is in scope despite the native-drop context.

assessments.summary: Reduce XamlCompositionBrushBase.CompositionBrush from public to protected to match the WinUI/WinAppSDK API surface (where it is protected).
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks any app/library that reads or writes XamlCompositionBrushBase.CompositionBrush from OUTSIDE a subclass (e.g. casting a custom brush and inspecting/assigning .CompositionBrush externally). On real WinUI this was never possible (it's protected), so most code already only touches it inside an OnConnected/OnDisconnected override of a derived brush, which protected still allows. Uno's wider-than-WinUI public surface means the rare consumer relying on the public getter/setter would get a compile error. Likelihood a typical app depends on external access is low; this is an advanced/niche scenario, but the property itself is on a commonly-subclassed type (custom composition brushes, AcrylicBrush).
assessments.notes: Confirmed in code: src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Media/XamlCompositionBrushBase.cs line 29 declares `public CompositionBrush CompositionBrush { get; set; }` (DP-backed via internal CompositionBrushProperty). WinUI/WinAppSDK declares this member as protected, so Uno's public is a divergence; BC17 aligns it. The change is mechanical: `public` -> `protected` on the property (lines 29-33). The internal CompositionBrushProperty DP and the .skia.cs OnPropertyChanged2 wiring are unaffected (they read it via GetValue/the property name internally, not via the public accessor). All in-repo usages set `CompositionBrush = ...` from inside subclasses: TestBrush.OnConnected (samples), AcrylicBrush.skia.cs (self), EffectBrushTests/LoadedImageSurfaceTests samples, and Given_XamlCompositionBrushBase runtime test OnConnected override -- all are subclass-internal and remain legal under protected. Grep for external `.CompositionBrush` member access in xaml.cs found none. NOT native-only: this is the cross-runtime/Skia public API surface of a brush type, not native rendering code being dropped. Danger 3 (not 2) because it's a protected-izing of a member on a subclassable public type that advanced users do extend; effort S. No GitHub PR reference and the code is still public, so it has not been done yet.

assessments.summary: Remove the public FeatureConfiguration.FrameworkElement.UseLegacyHitTest opt-in flag and its two legacy code paths, permanently locking hit-testing to the WinUI-aligned (default-off) behavior.
assessments.customerImpact: Only apps that explicitly set FeatureConfiguration.FrameworkElement.UseLegacyHitTest = true are affected; that opt-in restored Uno v3/v4-era semantics where any FrameworkElement (e.g. plain Control / custom element) with a non-null Background intercepted pointers. Removing it both deletes the public property (compile break for code referencing it) and forces WinUI-correct behavior, so a migrating app relying on the legacy hit-test would silently lose pointer interception on background-painted custom controls. Default is already false, so typical/new apps are unaffected. The flag is undocumented in /doc, signaling very low real-world usage.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should UseLegacyHitTest be hard-removed in this major, or marked [Obsolete] for one cycle first, given it is a public backward-compat escape hatch that migrating apps may still set?
assessments.notes: Confirmed in code: public static bool UseLegacyHitTest { get; set; } at FeatureConfiguration.cs:286 (default false). Two consumers: FrameworkElement.cs:830-838 IsViewHit() returns Background != null only when flag is on (the legacy path); and UIElement.Pointers.Managed.cs:89 a WASM-only (#if __WASM__) branch for externally-defined HTML elements. The managed hit-test path (UIElement.Pointers.Managed.cs, gated UNO_HAS_MANAGED_POINTERS) and IsViewHit/CoerceHitTestVisibility are used by Skia as well, not just native rendering, so this is NOT nativeOnly. Removal is a small, mechanical edit: drop the property and inline IsViewHit() to return false (matching current default) plus drop the WASM flag guard. No /doc references found. Danger kept at 2 (obscure default-off public compat flag almost no app touches) rather than 1, because it is a real public API surface and the path feeds Skia hit-testing.

assessments.summary: Remove the Uno-only `protected internal virtual FlyoutBase.Close()` method (and its overrides), redirecting all callers to the public WinUI `Hide()` it merely forwards to.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only consumers who subclass FlyoutBase/Flyout/MenuFlyout and either override or call the `protected` Close() — a niche scenario. Close() is an Uno invention not present in WinUI (the generated WinUI stub declares only Hide()), and it does nothing but call Hide(canCancel:true), identical to the public Hide(). Any code calling it via reflection/subclass should switch to Hide(). Typical apps and most libraries never touch it; in-repo all callers are internal (Button, Flyout, native MenuFlyout/NativeTimePickerFlyout) plus two tests, one already #if HAS_UNO-gated. Low probability of external dependency.
assessments.notes: FlyoutBase.Close() is `protected internal virtual` at FlyoutBase.cs:866 -> `Hide(canCancel: true)`. Not in WinUI: the generated stub src/Uno.UI/Generated/3.0.0.0/Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.Primitives/FlyoutBase.cs only declares Hide(). Public Hide() (FlyoutBase.cs:412) also calls Hide(canCancel:true), so Close() is a pure redundant duplicate. Overrides found: Flyout.cs:105 ("required for binary compatibility" comment), MenuFlyout.UIKit.cs:46 and MenuFlyout.Android.cs:57 (native-only, being dropped), NativeTimePickerFlyout.iOS.cs:166 (native iOS, being dropped). Internal callers to redirect to Hide(): Button.cs:45 (flyout.Close()), Flyout.cs:108 (base.Close()), Given_MenuFlyout.cs:430, Given_Flyout.cs:758 (already wrapped in #if HAS_UNO, confirming it is treated as an Uno-only API). Note PickerFlyoutAsyncOperation.cs:49 `Close()` is an unrelated IAsyncInfo member, not a FlyoutBase override. Watch the Flyout.cs "binary compatibility" comment — historically these `protected internal override` empty forwarders were added precisely to preserve a binary contract, so dropping Close() is a deliberate ABI break consistent with the major-version breaking pass. Effort S/mechanical; the only judgement call is whether to keep a temporary [Obsolete] shim, but per the breaking-change pass a clean removal is appropriate.

assessments.summary: Make FrameworkPropertyMetadata.DefaultUpdateSourceTrigger internal (or remove it), and consider making the whole FrameworkPropertyMetadata class internal.
assessments.customerImpact: The DefaultUpdateSourceTrigger property is a pure binary-compat stub with zero internal callers (the binding pipeline resolves the trigger independently in BindingExpression.ResolveUpdateSourceTrigger); hiding/removing it breaks essentially no one. Making the WHOLE class internal is broader: FrameworkPropertyMetadata is a WPF-shaped, Uno-only type (explicitly documented as NOT UWP-compatible) used by custom-control authors who manually register inheritable/weak-storage DPs (new FrameworkPropertyMetadata(...), .Options, HasWeakStorage). That is a niche advanced scenario most apps never touch, but library/control authors relying on it for inheritable DP metadata would break.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Scope: hide/remove only the dead DefaultUpdateSourceTrigger property (trivial, near-zero impact, danger 1-2), or also make the entire public FrameworkPropertyMetadata class internal (cuts off custom-control authors who manually register inheritable/weak-storage DPs)?
assessments.notes: Found at src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/FrameworkPropertyMetadata.cs:171. The property carries an in-code comment: "Kept for binary compat only. This property should be removed, and the whole FrameworkPropertyMetadata should be internal." — so this item is directly transcribed from that TODO. DefaultUpdateSourceTrigger has NO callers anywhere in the repo except its own declaration; the trigger default is computed independently in BindingExpression.cs (lines 339-349, same TextBox.TextProperty->Explicit logic), so the property is genuinely dead. Removing/internalizing just the property = legacy/dead-code cleanup, effort S, danger ~1-2. The "whole class internal" sub-proposal is materially larger: the public constructors are exercised by external-style code (XamlGenerationTests TestAttachedPropertyOwner etc.) and the WinAppSDKSyncGenerator emits `new FrameworkPropertyMetadata(...)`, so internalizing the class is a real breaking change for control-author code (bump to danger ~3, effort M) — hence needsDecision. Not native-rendering-only. No dependency on the DependencyObject-as-class item.

assessments.summary: The item asks to delete a "legacy member" at AutomationProperties.cs line ~44, but the member actually at that location is AnnotationsProperty, a legitimate public WinUI attached property (with read-only GetAnnotations accessor), not legacy code.
assessments.customerImpact: If taken literally and AnnotationsProperty/GetAnnotations were removed, any app or library setting Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Automation.AutomationProperties.AnnotationsProperty (or calling GetAnnotations) for accessibility annotations would break at compile/runtime. Usage is niche (advanced a11y scenarios), but it is a real WinUI-parity public API, so removal would be an unjustified breaking change. If, instead, the intent is the native-only OnAutomationIdChanged DOM/UIKit/Android branches in the sibling AutomationProperties.uno.cs, customer impact is nil (that native rendering path is being dropped anyway).
assessments.decisionQuestion: What is the actual "legacy member" meant here? Line ~44 of AutomationProperties.cs is AnnotationsProperty, a valid public WinUI attached property that should NOT be deleted. Did the line reference drift (the real target being the native-only OnAutomationIdChanged __APPLE_UIKIT__/__ANDROID__/__WASM__ branches in AutomationProperties.uno.cs), or a specific accessor/overload to drop?
assessments.notes: AutomationProperties.cs is a hand-written, WinUI-aligned a11y file authored Sep 2025 - Jan 2026 (commits 1c26d28207, 8a856ebd90, d0c46d0ccc); nothing here is "legacy." Exact line 44 = the </summary> doc line just above `public static DependencyProperty AnnotationsProperty`; the property body spans lines 45-50, accessor GetAnnotations at line 466. It correctly has only a getter (read-only attached, matching WinUI). The Generated stub (Generated/.../AutomationProperties.cs) at its line 44 is just a "// Skipping already declared method GetAutomationId" comment — no real code. No `legacy`/`obsolete`/`deprecated` markers exist in either file. The only genuinely native-rendering, droppable code in this type is the OnAutomationIdChanged native branches in AutomationProperties.uno.cs (UIView.AccessibilityIdentifier / AView.ContentDescription / WASM aria DOM) — if THAT is the intent, it is native-only (danger 1) and safe; but as literally written (line 44 of AutomationProperties.cs) it targets a public API and is danger 3 with a needed clarification. Could not confirm "already-done" because the description does not match any deletable legacy symbol at the cited location.

assessments.summary: Reduce the visibility of the menu-infrastructure helper interfaces IMenu, IMenuPresenter, and ISubMenuOwner (in Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls) from public to internal, matching WinUI where they live in the private IDL.
assessments.customerImpact: Very low. These are internal MenuFlyout/CommandBar plumbing interfaces (CloseSubMenu, OpenSubMenu, ParentOwner, SubPresenter, etc.). In WinUI they are declared in microsoft.ui.xaml.private.idl and are NOT public, so no app written against WinUI parity could legitimately depend on them. All implementers and consumers (MenuFlyout, MenuFlyoutPresenter, MenuFlyoutSubItem, SplitMenuFlyoutItem, CommandBar) live inside Uno.UI itself. A typical app or third-party library would essentially never reference these directly; only someone who hand-cast a control to one of these helper interfaces (an undocumented Uno-only accident) would break.
assessments.notes: Confirmed all three are currently declared `public partial interface` in src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Controls/MenuFlyout/{IMenu,IMenuPresenter,ISubMenuOwner}.cs (namespace Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls), so the change still applies. Verified in WinUI sources (D:/Work/microsoft-ui-xaml2/dxaml/.../microsoft.ui.xaml.private.idl lines 582/590/613) that these interfaces are in the PRIVATE IDL, i.e. internal in WinUI — Uno over-exposed them. All implementers/consumers are within Uno.UI (CommandBar, MenuFlyout, MenuFlyoutPresenter, MenuFlyoutSubItem, SplitMenuFlyoutItem), so internal is sufficient. The runtime test Given_SplitMenuFlyoutItem.When_Implements_ISubMenuOwner casts to ISubMenuOwner; it still compiles because Uno.UI/AssemblyInfo.cs has [InternalsVisibleTo("Uno.UI.RuntimeTests")]. Note: PackageDiffIgnore.xml already has an entry for MenuFlyoutSubItem.SetSubMenuDirection 'Moved to explicit ISubMenuOwner interface implementation', so the API-diff tooling will likely flag this visibility reduction and may need a PackageDiffIgnore entry. The IMenu matches in src/Uno.UWP and BaseActivityCallbacksGenerator are the unrelated Android.Views.IMenu type — not affected. Not native-only: these are Skia-relevant control interfaces, but the change is judged on normal merit (a safe, low-risk visibility tightening).

assessments.summary: Deprecate (and eventually remove) the VisualTreeHelper.AdaptNative/TryAdaptNative native-view-hosting code-path used to wrap raw Android.Views.View / UIKit.UIView instances into the managed visual tree on the native Android/iOS rendering targets.
assessments.customerImpact: Affects only apps that host RAW native views (Android.Views.View / UIKit.UIView) directly in the visual tree via VisualTreeHelper.AdaptNative()/TryAdaptNative(), the UIElementCollection.Add(nativeView) overload, Border.Add(View), or native-view XAML elements (xmlns:android/...:UIView) that the XAML generator wraps with AdaptNative. This is a niche, advanced interop path on the native targets being dropped; the supported alternative (assigning a native view to ContentControl/ContentPresenter.Content, and the modern Skia native-element hosting extension) remains. The public AdaptNative/TryAdaptNative symbols also exist on Skia/WASM/Reference where _View aliases to UIElement, but they are no-ops/throwers there, so deprecating them carries near-zero real breakage for Skia consumers. Typical apps do not use raw-native-view hosting.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should AdaptNative/TryAdaptNative just be marked [Obsolete]/[EditorBrowsable(Never)] now (keeping the symbols as no-ops on Skia for source compat) and the real wrapping logic deleted alongside the native Android/iOS targets, or should the public surface be removed entirely in this major version since the native rendering targets that gave it meaning are gone?
assessments.notes: AdaptNative(_View)/TryAdaptNative(_View) are public on VisualTreeHelper (VisualTreeHelper.cs:310,354). _View aliases to Android.Views.View / UIKit.UIView on native targets and to Microsoft.UI.Xaml.UIElement on the cross-runtime (#else) branch, so the method is meaningful ONLY on native Android/iOS; on Skia/WASM it would throw for a real FrameworkElement and the XAML-generator wrapper never fires (IsNativeView = IsAndroidView || IsIOSUIView, both native-only). All real callers are native-rendering code (UIElementCollectionExtensions.Xamarin.cs, ItemContainerHolderAdapter.Android.cs, MediaPlayerPresenter.Android.cs, Border.Add(View)). This is the LEGACY native-element hosting path and is entirely distinct from the modern Skia native-element hosting (ContentPresenter.skia.cs TryRegisterNativeElement/_nativeElementHostingExtension/SetAsNativeHostVisual) which BC23 does NOT touch. Code is fully present today and is the documented public way to host native views (doc/articles/native-views.md, plus xf-migration and apple-login interop docs), so the change still applies. danger=2 (public-surface deprecation of an obscure interop API, not a pure internal removal); nativeOnly=true because the actual hosting logic only serves native rendering. Docs (native-views.md, guides/xf-migration/native-controls.md, interop/apple-login.md) will need updating as part of the deprecation. Decision needed because the item is explicitly framed as a discussion ("weird edge cases, not officially supported except via ContentControl") and the obsolete-vs-remove scope is genuinely ambiguous.

assessments.summary: Add [EditorBrowsable(EditorBrowsableState.Never)] to the public Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Media.BrushConverter class so it stays public but is hidden from IntelliSense/object browser.
assessments.customerImpact: Effectively zero binary/source break: the type remains public, instantiable, and continues to function as the [TypeConverter] for Brush. EditorBrowsable only suppresses the type from IntelliSense/object-browser listings; it does not remove the API, change signatures, or affect runtime/XAML behavior. BrushConverter is almost never referenced directly by app code (it is consumed implicitly via the [TypeConverter(typeof(BrushConverter))] attribute on Brush and via XAML string-to-brush parsing), so practically no app or library depends on seeing it in completion. C# compilers also ignore EditorBrowsable for symbols defined in the same solution, only honoring it from referenced assemblies, so even the in-repo XAML generators/tests are unaffected.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: BrushConverter.cs declares `public partial class BrushConverter : TypeConverter` in namespace Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Media with NO [EditorBrowsable] attribute present, so the change still applies. The change is a one-attribute addition (add `using System.ComponentModel;` already present at line 4, then `[EditorBrowsable(EditorBrowsableState.Never)]` above the class at line 10). The EditorBrowsable-Never pattern is already established across the codebase (60+ usages, incl. ImageSource.Android.cs in the same folder). BrushConverter is referenced as the TypeConverter for Brush (Brush.cs line 16) and registered in LinkerDefinition.Wasm.xml so it is kept by the linker. It does NOT exist in the WinUI C++ sources (D:/Work/microsoft-ui-xaml2/src dxaml/controls grep: no hits) -- it is a managed projection/convenience type, so this is an Uno-side polish to declutter the public surface rather than a WinUI-parity requirement. Low danger (2, not 1) only because it touches a genuinely public type rather than purely internal/native-rendering code; functionally it is a no-op for consumers. No GitHub reference; no dependencies on other items.

assessments.summary: Reduce visibility of (or remove) the Uno-only public Border.ChildProperty DependencyProperty so Border's public API matches WinUI, where Child has no public backing DP.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only apps/libraries that explicitly reference Border.ChildProperty by name — e.g. SetBinding(Border.ChildProperty, ...), a style Setter targeting it, RegisterPropertyChangedCallback, or GetValue/SetValue(Border.ChildProperty). This is uncommon: Border.Child is virtually always set directly via the CLR property or XAML content, not via the DP field. WinUI users never had this field, so portable code already avoids it. Low real-world dependency.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should Border.ChildProperty be made internal (keep the DP, hide the field) to match WinUI, or fully removed? Internal is the safer parity fix since the DP must still exist to drive measure/arrange/transition logic; full removal would require rewriting Child to a plain CLR property with separate change tracking.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: Border.ChildProperty is declared `public static DependencyProperty` at src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Controls/Border/Border.cs:101, in the public Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.Border. WinUI has NO public Border.ChildProperty — verified via the Generated stub (src/Uno.UI/Generated/3.0.0.0/Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls/Border.cs): it lists `// Skipping already declared property Child` but has NO `ChildProperty` skip line, whereas Popup's stub explicitly emits `// Skipping already declared property ChildProperty` because WinUI genuinely exposes Popup.ChildProperty. So the generator confirms WinUI metadata exposes Child (CLR) but not ChildProperty (DP) for Border. (Local WinUI mirror at D:\Work\microsoft-ui-xaml2 has no Border IDL/native sources, but the generated-stub diff is conclusive.) Internal usage is minimal: only Border.cs's own Child accessor (lines 85/97) and one unit test (Given_DependencyProperty.cs:1621) reference Border.ChildProperty; ItemContainer.ChildProperty and Popup.ChildProperty are separate symbols. Recommended fix: change `public` to `internal` on the field. NOT native-only — Border.cs is the shared cross-platform control. Pairs naturally with any broader "drop Uno-only public DP fields that don't exist in WinUI" cleanup.

assessments.summary: Change Microsoft.UI.Xaml.DependencyObject from a public interface (implemented via the DependencyObjectGenerator mixin) into a real base class on the cross-runtime targets, so UIElement and all DO-derived types inherit it directly instead of having GetValue/SetValue/store members injected.
assessments.customerImpact: Foundational: every control and DP-bearing type derives from DependencyObject, so this is a binary-breaking type-system change touching the whole hierarchy. But realistic source breakage for a typical app is narrow: using DependencyObject as a base/parameter/return type behaves identically whether it is a class or interface, and the move is TOWARD WinUI parity (WinUI's DependencyObject is a class), so code written against WinUI semantics breaks LESS. Breaks concentrate on advanced/library consumers that (a) hand-implement the DependencyObject interface on a non-derived type, (b) depend on interface-style multiple-inheritance, or (c) reflect/source-gen over its interface shape. Binary-breaking for all recompiled-against assemblies regardless.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Now that the native Android/iOS/WASM-DOM targets are being dropped (removing the native-view inheritance constraint that forced DependencyObject to be an interface), should DependencyObject become a class on ALL remaining (Skia) targets — and does the generated mixin/IDependencyObjectStoreProvider machinery get retired into the base class, or kept as-is with the class merely re-rooting the hierarchy?
assessments.notes: Confirmed in-repo: src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/DependencyObject.cs declares `public partial interface DependencyObject` with NO platform #if guard, so it is currently an interface on every target including Skia/Wasm/reference -> change still applies, nothing already done. The interface exists solely because native mobile UIElement must inherit a native view: UIElement.Android.cs derives BindableView, UIElement.UIKit.cs derives BindableUIView, whereas UIElement.skia.cs / .wasm.cs / .reference.cs already do `: DependencyObject` directly. The DependencyObjectGenerator injects IDependencyObjectStoreProvider/IDependencyObjectInternal/IWeakReferenceProvider + GetValue/SetValue/store into each DO-derived class. Issue #17099 is OPEN, stale, scoped to Skia/Wasm and predates the native-target drop — the drop REMOVES the only reason for the interface, so the change is more natural and lower-risk than the issue framing implies, though still a foundational binary-breaking re-rooting (hence danger 4, effort L: requires reworking the generator to stop emitting the mixin where the base class now provides it, and validating the whole control hierarchy + serialization/binder partials). needsDecision because the correct end-state design (class on all targets vs Skia/Wasm only; how much of the generated mixin folds into the base) is genuinely ambiguous and should be confirmed with the maintainer. nativeOnly=false: touches core cross-runtime Uno.UI type system, not native rendering code being deleted.

assessments.summary: Change Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Media.DoubleCollection from `: List<double>` to a composition-based class (private List<double> field) implementing IList<double>, matching WinUI and the sibling PointCollection.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks any code that relies on List<double>-only members exposed today via inheritance (AddRange, Sort, RemoveAll, FindIndex, BinarySearch, ForEach, GetRange, Capacity, ToArray-as-List-method, etc.) or that upcasts a Shape.StrokeDashArray to `List<double>` / passes it to an API expecting `List<double>`. StrokeDashArray itself is moderately common, but depending on the List<double> identity rather than IList<double> is uncommon in typical app/library code — most consumers only enumerate, index, or set it from a string. So real-world breakage is limited but non-zero.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Media/DoubleCollection.cs line 9 still declares `public partial class DoubleCollection : List<double>, IList<double>, IEnumerable<double>` — change not yet done. This aligns with WinUI (where DoubleCollection is a sealed runtime class implementing IList<double>, NOT deriving from List<T>) and with the sibling PointCollection in the same folder, which already uses composition (private List<Point> _points, implements IList<Point>) — PointCollection is the ready-made template. Effort is S: mirror PointCollection. Internal repo usages are all safe under IList<double>: Shape.cs uses only the DP get/set; Shape.wasm.cs uses .ToArray()/Count (IEnumerable/ICollection); FastConvert uses the implicit string->DoubleCollection operator; runtime test (Given_XamlReader.When_DoubleCollection) uses indexer+Count. The implicit operators (string and double[]) and the IEnumerable<double> ctor must be preserved. Note: PointCollection internally uses a List<Action> callback list (`event Action`-style) which AGENTS.md discourages; if cloning that template, prefer a proper EventHandler-based change notification for the new DoubleCollection rather than copying the Action pattern. Not native-only (this is shared Uno.UI media code used by Skia). No dependency on BC26 (DependencyObject-as-class).

assessments.summary: Make Microsoft.UI.Composition.CompositionSpriteShape.StrokeDashArray a get-only, non-nullable property (lazily-created mutable collection) to match WinUI, removing the public setter and the nullable return type.
assessments.customerImpact: Affects the public Composition API CompositionSpriteShape.StrokeDashArray. Two source-breaking changes: (1) removing the setter breaks any code doing `spriteShape.StrokeDashArray = arr;` — but WinUI never had a setter, so portable WinUI code never used it and only Uno-specific code could be affected; (2) making the return non-nullable could break callers that null-check the result, though WinUI returns non-null so this aligns behavior. CompositionSpriteShape is a low-level Composition primitive that very few app developers touch directly (Shapes use it internally), so real-world breakage is rare. The only in-repo consumer is the internal Shape.skia.cs.
assessments.notes: Confirmed divergence from WinUI. Current Uno impl (src/Uno.UI.Composition/Composition/CompositionSpriteShape.cs:91-95) declares `public CompositionStrokeDashArray? StrokeDashArray { get => _strokeDashArray; set => SetProperty(...) }` with a nullable backing field (line 18). WinUI's generated contract at src/Uno.UWP/Generated/3.0.0.0/Windows.UI.Composition/CompositionSpriteShape.cs:188 lists only `StrokeDashArray.get` (no `.set`), unlike every sibling property which lists both get+set, proving the WinUI property is get-only and returns a non-null lazily-created collection that callers mutate in place. Implementation work: change to get-only non-null, lazily instantiate the CompositionStrokeDashArray (which needs a Compositor — available via base class), and rewrite the sole internal consumer Shape.skia.cs UpdateStrokeDashArray() (lines 74-90) which currently assigns `_shape.StrokeDashArray = null` (line 80) or a fresh instance (line 89) — instead Clear()+Add() into the existing collection, removing the null-assignment branch. Not native-only: this lives in the shared Uno.UI.Composition layer used by Skia (the .skia.cs partial drives rendering). Skia consumers read StrokeDashArray via pattern `is { Count: > 0 }` so non-null default empty collection is fine. Danger 2: public Composition surface but an obscure low-level primitive app devs almost never set directly; WinUI parity means portable code is unaffected.

assessments.summary: Remove the public static Window.BypassCheckToCloseKeyboard property and its associated keyboard-dismissal branch in the native UIKit UIWindow.HitTest override.
assessments.customerImpact: Technically a public API removal: `Uno.UI.Controls.Window.BypassCheckToCloseKeyboard` is a public static property on the native iOS/tvOS UIWindow subclass. In practice nobody depends on it — it is an undocumented native-iOS-only hit-test tuning flag (Impediment #19821) that already defaults to true, has zero references anywhere in the repo (no samples, no docs, no other code), and only gates a native-view-hierarchy keyboard-dismissal path in HitTest. With the default of true that branch is already inert (falls straight through to base.HitTest). The whole construct lives in the native UIKit rendering layer being dropped, so customer breakage is effectively nil.
assessments.notes: The description's filename "Window.iOS.cs" is inaccurate — there is no Window.iOS.cs. The actual symbol lives in src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Window/Native/NativeWindow.UIKit.cs (class `Uno.UI.Controls.Window : UIWindow`, partial), compiled for __APPLE_UIKIT__ (native iOS/tvOS/MacCatalyst). The property is at line 33 (already `= true`), and the only consumer is the `if (!BypassCheckToCloseKeyboard && uievent is { Type: UIEventType.Touches })` branch in the HitTest override (lines 171-223). The "align to true" goal is already done behaviorally (default is true); what remains is the actual removal of the property plus collapsing the now-dead HitTest branch (and dropping the now-unused _touchTrace/_trace/TraceProvider touch-trace plumbing it used). Since Skia-on-iOS uses the generic netX Uno.UI assembly (not these .UIKit.cs files), this is pure native-rendering-layer cleanup. Strictly a public-surface removal, so it must still be listed as a breaking change, but danger is 1 given it is native-drop code with no real consumers.

assessments.summary: Delete the native-iOS-only WindowView (UIView subclass that rotated the window per status-bar orientation), which is dropped along with the native iOS/UIKit rendering target.
assessments.customerImpact: Effectively nobody. WindowView is a native iOS/UIKit host UIView subclass (Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.WindowView : UIView) that only handled status-bar-orientation rotation. It is compiled solely for the native iOS target being removed, and it has ZERO references anywhere in the repo (no .cs, no XAML instantiates or consumes it) — it is already orphaned. Although technically public, no realistic app/library directly news up or subtypes this internal host plumbing.
assessments.notes: File confirmed present at src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Controls/WindowView.iOS.cs (note actual casing is .iOS.cs, the item description wrote .ios.cs). Class is `public partial class WindowView : UIKit.UIView` — references ObjCRuntime/Foundation/UIKit/CoreGraphics, pure native-iOS code via the .iOS.cs suffix (compiled only for the native iOS/UIKit target, which is being dropped). Grep for the symbol `WindowView` across the whole worktree returns exactly one hit: the file itself. No XAML references either. So this is both native-only AND already dead code internally — removal is the safest possible class of change (danger 1). Skia-on-iOS does not use the native .iOS.cs path, so no Skia regression. Trivial single-file delete; no API surface anyone reaches.

assessments.summary: Delete the native-Android-only diagnostic stub class Uno.UI.DataBinding.BinderDetails and the generator code that emits the do-nothing GetBinderDetail() method on native Android view subclasses.
assessments.customerImpact: Effectively nobody. BinderDetails is a public class but every member is a no-op returning null, it exists only on the native Android target (.Android.cs, inherits Java.Lang.Object), and the generated GetBinderDetail() it pairs with also just returns null. It is internal-style memory-diagnostics scaffolding that real apps don't call. The native Android view target it lives on is being dropped anyway, so any consumer is being removed regardless. Note: this does NOT touch the separate, broader BinderReferenceHolder class (cross-platform diagnostics), which only references "BinderDetails" cosmetically in a DebuggerDisplay string.
assessments.notes: BinderDetails.Android.cs is Android-only (file suffix + Java.Lang.Object base). All 4 methods + ctor are no-ops returning null; IsBinderDetailsEnabled is an unused flag. Generator coupling: DependencyObjectGenerator.WriteAndroidBinderDetails (called at ~line 199, defined ~line 453) emits `public BinderDetails GetBinderDetail() { return null; }` only when the type derives from Android.Views.View (_androidViewSymbol) — i.e. native Android views, not Skia (.skia.cs) compilations. Removal = delete the file + remove WriteAndroidBinderDetails and its call site. Verified no other usages in the repo (only 3 files mention the symbol). BinderReferenceHolder is unrelated and stays. Safe danger-1 native-rendering-layer cleanup; could even be folded into the broader native-Android-view removal sweep. Optional dependency: sequence after whatever does the bulk DependencyObjectGenerator native-Android pruning to avoid merge churn, but it's self-contained enough to land standalone.

assessments.summary: Delete the inert public stub class Microsoft.UI.Composition.DelegatedInkTrailVisual (Visual subclass with only a private ctor, no public members, no factory, zero consumers).
assessments.customerImpact: Removes a public WinUI type name (Microsoft.UI.Composition.DelegatedInkTrailVisual). In Uno it is a hollow stub: derives from Visual, has only a private constructor, exposes no public members, and there is no Compositor factory to instantiate it (no CreateDelegatedInkTrailVisual anywhere in the repo). No app can functionally use it, so no behavior breaks. The only conceivable break is binary/source API-compat: code that does typeof(DelegatedInkTrailVisual), reflection over the type, or API-diff tooling that asserts the type's presence. Vanishingly unlikely for a typical app or library.
assessments.notes: Located one real definition: src/Uno.UI.Composition/Composition/DelegatedInkTrailVisual.cs - a public partial class DelegatedInkTrailVisual : Visual with a single private DelegatedInkTrailVisual(Compositor) ctor (IDE0051-suppressed as "Required constructor for base class") and no other members. git grep across all of src/ finds zero references to the type, DelegatedInkTrailVisual, InkTrailVisual, or any CreateDelegatedInkTrailVisual factory outside its own file - it is genuinely dead public API surface. PackageDiffIgnore.xml (Group 11a, lines 2241-2245) ALREADY suppresses the diff for the LEGACY Windows.UI.Composition.DelegatedInkTrailVisual projection removal, and explicitly notes the "real Uno impls live in Microsoft.UI.Composition ... and are preserved on the branch." So this BC removes the still-preserved Microsoft.UI.Composition copy. NOT native-rendering-only: this lives in Uno.UI.Composition (the cross-runtime/Skia composition layer), not in native-target view code, hence nativeOnly=false - but it is dead-code regardless. Effort S: delete the one file; if a package-diff entry is needed for the Microsoft.UI.Composition.DelegatedInkTrailVisual removal, extend the existing Group-11a regex (currently it only matches the Windows.UI.Composition.* variant). No dependency on DependencyObject-as-class or any other BC. Danger pegged at 2 (not 1) only because it is genuinely public surface in a shipping assembly rather than internal/native-only, so strict binary-compat checkers could flag it - functional risk is effectively nil.

assessments.summary: Remove the public FeatureConfiguration.UIElement.UseInvalidateMeasurePath and UseInvalidateArrangePath feature flags (both default true) and hard-code the dirty-path layout optimization, deleting the legacy full-propagation fallback branches.
assessments.customerImpact: Both are public static props on FeatureConfiguration.UIElement defaulting to true (the kept behavior). Breaks only an app/library that explicitly assigns the flag (almost always to false to opt out of the dirty-path optimization, a niche perf/diagnostic escape hatch) - that line stops compiling. Typical apps never touch these. No runtime behavior change for default consumers since true is already the shipping default. The related per-element FrameworkElementHelper.SetUseMeasurePathDisabled API is separate and not being removed here, but its doc comments reference these flags.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Is the always-true dirty-path layout optimization now considered correct/safe enough on all retained targets (Skia generic + Skia-on-native) that the false fallback can be deleted outright, or should the flag be kept (or first deprecated with [EditorBrowsable]) as a regression escape hatch for a release?
assessments.notes: Confirmed in code: FeatureConfiguration is public static (FeatureConfiguration.cs:18), nested UIElement is public static (:708); both flags are public static bool = true (:714, :720). Read at branch points: UIElement.Layout.crossruntime.cs:40,107,202; UIElement.skia.cs:271; UIElement.wasm.cs:369,400 (native WASM path); ILayouterElement.cs:40 (native Android/iOS layouter). Removal means keeping the true branch and deleting else/fallback paths plus the dirty_path-disabled-globally OR-clauses (crossruntime.cs:202, ILayouterElement.cs:40). Two SamplesApp perf/diagnostic samples toggle these and would need updating. Not native-only: the crossruntime + skia paths are retained and consumed by Skia, so nativeOnly=false. Effort M because the logic must be hand-collapsed at ~7 call sites across measure and arrange, plus sample fixups; mechanical but spread out. Per-element IsMeasureDirtyPathDisabled / FrameworkElementHelper.Set*PathDisabled API is orthogonal and not in scope for this item.

assessments.summary: Remove TextBox's empty no-op override of OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged, and (decision) narrow the base ContentPresenter.OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged from protected virtual to private protected.
assessments.customerImpact: Two-part. (1) Removing TextBox's empty override mainly changes internal behavior; a TextBox subclass that called base.OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged is essentially nonexistent in the wild, so near-zero breakage. (2) Narrowing ContentPresenter.OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged from `protected virtual` to `private protected` is a compile-breaking change for any external subclass of ContentPresenter (public, non-sealed) that overrides or calls this method. This is an Uno-only extensibility hook (WinUI's ContentPresenter has no such virtual), tied to Uno's DP changed-callback, so it is extremely obscure and almost no app/library would touch it. Realistic impact: very low, advanced-user-only.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should the base ContentPresenter.OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged be narrowed from `protected virtual` to `private protected` (sealing it off as an internal-only hook), or just removed entirely / kept protected? The TextBox override should be deleted either way; the open question is the base member's final visibility.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in code: TextBox.cs lines 1410-1412 carry a self-documented `// TODO: Remove as a breaking change for Uno 6` plus `// Also, make OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged private protected.`, and the override body is empty `{ }` (deliberately suppresses base behavior). The only `protected virtual OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged` declaration in the repo is ContentPresenter.cs line 538, invoked from the VerticalContentAlignmentProperty changed-callback (line 534) with a partial hook OnVerticalContentAlignmentChangedPartial (line 543). NOTE on hierarchy: TextBox derives from Control : FrameworkElement, NOT ContentPresenter, yet it `override`s the ContentPresenter virtual and the repo compiles — I could not locate the matching base virtual on Control/FrameworkElement (Control's VerticalContentAlignment property is declared Uno-side but no OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged virtual was found in the Control/* partials or generated stubs). This is worth a maintainer sanity-check, but does not change the triage: the concrete edits are deleting the TextBox override and deciding the base member's visibility. Change is cross-platform Uno.UI core control code (compiled for Skia + all targets), so nativeOnly=false. Effort S (a few lines). danger 3: visibility reduction of a protected virtual on a public, non-sealed control (ContentPresenter), though it is an Uno-only, very obscure hook.

assessments.summary: Delete the public FeatureConfiguration.Binding.IgnoreINPCSameReferences flag and the three conditional branches in BindingPath.BindingItem.cs, making the binding engine always take the default (false) "correct" code path.
assessments.customerImpact: Almost nobody breaks. The flag defaults to false, is never set anywhere in the repo, and the code comments explicitly state false is the correct behavior — so removing it is behavior-neutral for the default case. The only affected consumers are advanced apps that explicitly set Binding.IgnoreINPCSameReferences = true to opt into the legacy reference-equality short-circuit (a path the comments flag as a known bug: a sub-property change could be missed). Those apps lose a compile-time symbol (build break, easy to delete the line) and gain correct INPC re-evaluation. Realistically a vanishingly small fraction of apps touch this niche FeatureConfiguration flag.
assessments.notes: Flag found at src/Uno.UI/FeatureConfiguration.cs:352 (public static bool inside public static class FeatureConfiguration.Binding). Used in exactly 3 spots, all in src/Uno.UI/DataBinding/BindingPath.BindingItem.cs (lines 64, 197, 380). Defaults to false; no assignment to true anywhere in repo; no runtime/unit-test references. Code comment at BindingPath.BindingItem.cs:195-196 documents false as the correct behavior and explicitly says "In Uno 6, we should remove the following line" — so this removal is a pre-planned, decision-already-made cleanup for the major version, not an open question. Removal = delete the property plus collapse the three `IgnoreINPCSameReferences ?` branches to their always-taken (false) form: (1) drop the early-return DataContext equality short-circuit at line 64, (2) drop the `&& (!Ignore... || previousValue != newValue)` guard at line 197 so RaiseValueChanged always fires, (3) unconditionally compute valueHandler.PreviousValue at line 380 (minor: this re-introduces a GetSourceValue user-code call that the flag was avoiding — negligible).
