Cross-platform binding engine (Uno.UI), not native rendering, hence nativeOnly=false; but danger is 1 because it's an internal opt-in legacy-compat flag, default-off, with a documented intent to remove.

assessments.summary: Reduce visibility of the Uno-specific `ContentPresenter.ContentTemplateRoot` property (currently `public` getter with `protected` setter) down from public, ideally to `internal`, since it is not part of the WinUI surface.
assessments.customerImpact: Affects only apps/libraries that read `ContentPresenter.ContentTemplateRoot` directly. This is a Uno-only extension (WinUI exposes `ContentTemplateRoot` on ContentControl, NOT on ContentPresenter — confirmed by PackageDiffIgnore.xml listing the ContentPresenter getter/setter as Uno "Api alignments"). Most consumers use ContentControl.ContentTemplateRoot, which stays public. A typical app is very unlikely to depend on the ContentPresenter variant; advanced control authors grabbing a presenter's realized template root are the only plausible breakers. Note the protected setter is also part of the public surface for subclassers of ContentPresenter.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should `ContentPresenter.ContentTemplateRoot` be made `private` or `internal`? The issue says "make it private", but in-assembly callers (HyperlinkButton.mux.cs, FrameworkElement.cs native, ButtonBase.UIKit/Android) read it cross-type, so `private` would break compilation — `internal` is the realistic minimum. Also decide whether to keep a PackageDiffIgnore entry vs. fully removing the public-API-diff exception.
assessments.notes: Definition at ContentPresenter.cs:859 — `public View ContentTemplateRoot { get; protected set; }`. The issue's claim "Only ContentPresenter makes calls to it" is inaccurate: external readers exist at HyperlinkButton.mux.cs:146 and FrameworkElement.cs:926 (XAMARIN-gated), plus ButtonBase.UIKit.cs/ButtonBase.Android.cs (native-only). All are inside Uno.UI, so `internal` keeps them compiling; `private` does not. Several of those external callers (ButtonBase.UIKit/Android, FrameworkElement XAMARIN path) are native-target rendering code being dropped, which would simplify the cut, but HyperlinkButton.mux.cs is cross-platform — so this is NOT native-only overall. Note: ContentControl also has its own separate public ContentTemplateRoot (ContentControl.cs:215) which is a real WinUI API and is OUT of scope for this item. WinAppSDKSyncGenerator/SymbolMatchingHelpers.cs match was unrelated boilerplate. Marked OPEN, labeled difficulty/starter + kind/breaking-change.

assessments.summary: Remove the XAML source-generator fallback that lets a Style `<Setter Property="X" Value="...">` target a plain CLR (non-DependencyProperty) property by emitting a `Setter<T>` with a property-assignment action; after removal such XAML fails generation with "'X' is not a DependencyProperty".
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks (at XAML compile time, not runtime) any app or library whose Style contains a `<Setter Property="...">` whose target is a plain CLR property rather than a DependencyProperty — an Uno-only tolerance that WinUI never allowed. Uncommon in typical apps (most setter targets are DPs), but a few do exist (e.g. Uno's own ComboBox.IsPopupFullscreen sample relies on it). Failure mode is a clear, actionable XAML generation error pointing at the offending property; fix is to convert the property to a DependencyProperty. Public `Setter<T>` type and the C#-facing non-DP setter API are untouched; only the implicit XAML-side fallback is dropped.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in this repo: XamlFileGenerator.cs lines 6444-6456 carry the explicit marker `// TODO: Remove this else if in Uno 6 as a breaking change.` plus the `IsLegacySetter` helper at 6548-6558 (`!IsDependencyProperty(...)`). This is the exact code added by PR #16134 (merge commit 7e6f3956de, "Restore non-DP support in setters" fb64c17437). Removing the else-if makes a non-DP setter fall through to the normal path; the property is then resolved as a DependencyProperty and `BuildDependencyProperty` (line 5587-5592) already throws XamlGenerationException "'X' is not a DependencyProperty in type 'Y'", so the error path is already wired — low-effort deletion. Also delete the IsLegacySetter helper and update the ComboBox_FullScreen_Popup.xaml sample (or make IsPopupFullscreen a real DP — currently a plain `public bool IsPopupFullscreen { get; set; }` in ComboBox.custom.cs:485). Public `Setter<T>` class (Setter.Generic.cs) and `ICSharpPropertySetter` are NOT removed by this item — they're still used by C# code and unit tests (Given_ComboBox / Given_ItemsControl use `new Setter<...>`), so keep them. Not native-rendering-only; this is the cross-platform XAML codegen, so it affects all targets equally. Effort S: a focused deletion plus one sample fix and golden-output/runtime-test verification. Validation done by code review/inspection only — not compiled or run.

assessments.summary: Remove the Background/BackgroundProperty DependencyProperty from FrameworkElement (and the internal IFrameworkElement interface) and declare it on Control, matching WinUI where FrameworkElement has no Background; to preserve behavior it must also be re-declared on the non-Control FrameworkElement subclasses that paint a background today (Panel, Border, ContentPresenter, TextBlock, RichTextBlock, Shape, Glyphs, CalendarViewBaseItem).
assessments.customerImpact: Background is currently inherited by every FrameworkElement. After the move, any app/library code that reads or sets .Background through a FrameworkElement-typed (or Panel/Border/Grid/StackPanel/TextBlock-typed) reference breaks at compile time unless that exact type re-declares the property. XAML <Setter Property="Background"> in styles targeting non-Control types, and code-behind like grid.Background or border.Background, are extremely common, so a typical app almost certainly depends on this. If Uno only adds it to Control (and forgets Panel/Border/ContentPresenter/TextBlock/Shape), those very widely used controls silently lose Background entirely. Mitigated only insofar as the internal render path uses IBorderInfoProvider.Background (each border type implements its own), not FrameworkElement.Background, so rendering keeps working once each type re-declares the DP.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Do we move Background to Control AND independently re-declare it on every non-Control type that paints a background (Panel, Border, ContentPresenter, TextBlock, RichTextBlock, Shape, Glyphs, CalendarViewBaseItem) to match WinUI exactly, or keep the convenient FrameworkElement-level Background to avoid a wide, app-breaking source change? If we proceed, confirm the full list of non-Control types that must re-declare it so none silently loses Background.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in-repo: Background/BackgroundProperty is declared on the internal IFrameworkElement interface (IFrameworkElement.cs line 98) and implemented on FrameworkElement per-platform (FrameworkElement.Interface.skia.cs line 83/91 manual Register; .wasm.cs line 97-104 [GeneratedDependencyProperty]; .reference.cs line 72-80). Generated Control.cs explicitly emits "Skipping already declared property Background" / "Skipping already declared property BackgroundProperty" because it is inherited from FE, proving Control has no independent declaration today. Control.cs only overrides OnBackgroundChanged with a no-op (the FE-level brush is just an inheritance placeholder for children). Border, ContentPresenter, Panel all reference BackgroundProperty (Border.cs:334, ContentPresenter.cs:1149, Panel.cs:247) but none declare it; they derive from FrameworkElement, not Control (confirmed: Border:FrameworkElement, ContentPresenter:FrameworkElement, Shape:FrameworkElement, TextBlock:FrameworkElement). Good news for the internal refactor: the border-rendering pipeline reads IBorderInfoProvider.Background (BorderLayerState.cs), so rendering is decoupled from FE-level Background and will keep working once each border type re-declares the DP. This is a WinUI-parity correction (WinUI puts Background on Control/Panel/Border/ContentPresenter/TextBlock individually, never on FrameworkElement). Danger 4 not 5: it is a base-class member relocation affecting many controls and common public usage, but not a whole-type-system semantic change like DependencyObject-as-class. Not native-only: it touches Skia public API and render path. No GitHub reference found; no extant attempt in the tree.

assessments.summary: Rename the public DependencyPropertyValuePrecedences enum member ExplicitStyle to ExplicitOrImplicitStyle, repurpose ImplicitStyle's role onto DefaultStyle, and mark the now-redundant TemplatedParent/ExplicitStyle/ImplicitStyle members as [EditorBrowsable(Never)] back-compat aliases, while cleaning up the matching internal call sites and stale "#15684" TODO comments.
assessments.customerImpact: Affects only advanced users who call the public DependencyObjectExtensions.SetValue/GetValue(..., DependencyPropertyValuePrecedences?) overloads or compare GetCurrentHighestValuePrecedence results against a specific precedence value - a niche, internals-aware scenario most apps never touch. The PR was deliberately written to stay source/binary compatible: old member names (ExplicitStyle, ImplicitStyle, TemplatedParent) are retained as [EditorBrowsable(Never)] aliases with pinned numeric values, so existing code still compiles and runs. The only visible change is ExplicitStyle disappearing from IntelliSense in favor of ExplicitOrImplicitStyle (semantic rename), and the previously-undocumented ImplicitStyle precedence behavior shifting to DefaultStyle. The internal Stack-array-size-16-to-8 effect is invisible to consumers.
assessments.decisionQuestion: For this major version, do we keep the PR's compat-preserving approach ([EditorBrowsable(Never)] aliases for ExplicitStyle/ImplicitStyle/TemplatedParent), or - since this is a breaking-changes release - hard-remove the obsolete members for a cleaner public enum?
assessments.notes: Confirmed NOT done: enum at DependencyPropertyValuePrecedences.cs still has old members (ExplicitStyle, ImplicitStyle, TemplatedParent) with no [EditorBrowsable], and the stale "NOTE: ... will be fixed with https://github.com/unoplatform/uno/pull/15684/" comments are still present at DependencyObjectStore.cs:2078 & 2085 and DependencyPropertyHelper.cs:176, plus the "(which is otherwise unused)" comment at FrameworkElement.cs:778. The two files that grep-matched ExplicitOrImplicitStyle only contain it inside those TODO comments, not as enum members. PR #15684 is CLOSED (not merged); linked issue #16356 ("DependencyProperties precedences storage is wrong") is still OPEN, so the underlying precedence-semantics bug remains. DependencyPropertyValuePrecedences is a public enum in Microsoft.UI.Xaml consumed by public SetValue/GetValue extension overloads, so it IS public surface - but the PR's design (hidden aliases) makes the change effectively non-breaking, hence danger 2. The .tt mixin edits (Android/UIKit) are pure enum-name updates needed to keep those native targets compiling; not the substance of the change. PackageDiffIgnore.xml edit was just a BOM strip. This is a cleanup riding on top of a deeper precedence-storage fix (issue #16356) - shipping the rename without also fixing the storage logic only renames the symptom; whether to bundle the actual behavior fix is the real design question.

assessments.summary: Delete the entire Uno.UI.Controls.Legacy ListViewBase folder (legacy native-only ListView/GridView/HorizontalListView/HorizontalGridView and their iOS/Android sources/layouts), which only exists in the native Android and iOS/UIKit assemblies.
assessments.customerImpact: Affects only apps that explicitly use the long-deprecated Uno-specific Uno.UI.Controls.Legacy.ListView/GridView/HorizontalListView/HorizontalGridView native controls (xmlns "using:Uno.UI.Controls.Legacy"), and only on native Android or native iOS/UIKit targets. These predate the standard WinUI ListView/GridView and the cross-platform virtualizing list; the in-repo samples flag them "deprecated"/"Uno-specific, no Windows sample". With native rendering targets dropped, the types do not exist on Skia at all, so no Skia app can depend on them. Vanishingly few apps use them; standard ListView/GridView are unaffected.
assessments.notes: Confirmed: every file in src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Controls/ListViewBase/Legacy is a .Android.cs or .UIKit.cs/.Legacy.UIKit.cs source. The controls derive from native base classes (Android.Widget.ListView; BindableUICollectionView : UICollectionView) and live in the public namespace Uno.UI.Controls.Legacy. They are tracked as public API in build/PackageDiffIgnore.xml, but those entries only describe the native Android/iOS assemblies being dropped. No Skia/.crossruntime/.reference variant exists, so this code is gone from every retained target regardless. Cleanup also needs the legacy SamplesApp XAML samples removed (gated under not_win native xmlns) and GridExtensions.cs (internal static, namespace Uno.UI.Controls.Legacy, View=UIElement) which supports these controls. The ProgressRing/Legacy folder shares the same namespace but is a separate control and out of scope for BC40. Danger 1: native-only rendering removal; not auto-safe only if these were Uno.UWP/Foundation APIs, which they are not. Effort S-to-M depending on how thoroughly the SamplesApp legacy samples and PackageDiffIgnore entries are pruned.

assessments.summary: Change VisualInteractionSource.TryRedirectForManipulation's parameter from Windows.UI.Input.PointerPoint to Microsoft.UI.Input.PointerPoint to match WinUI exactly, removing the documented Uno-6 divergence comment block.
assessments.customerImpact: VisualInteractionSource.TryRedirectForManipulation is a public WinUI-surface API, but a niche one (custom InteractionTracker / pull-to-refresh / scroll redirection scenarios). The signature flips the PointerPoint namespace to the correct WinUI one (Microsoft.UI.Input). It is a binary-breaking change, but source-compatible for the overwhelmingly common call shape vis.TryRedirectForManipulation(e.GetCurrentPoint(null)) because GetCurrentPoint already returns Microsoft.UI.Input.PointerPoint, and implicit conversion operators between the two PointerPoint types already exist in both directions (Uno.UI/UI/Input/WinRT/PointerPoint.cs). The only callers who could break at source level are those who declared a local explicitly typed as Windows.UI.Input.PointerPoint, which is rare. Typical apps/libraries do not call this API at all.
assessments.notes: The item's "legacy stub at lines 83-89" no longer matches current line numbers (file has since grown to 105 lines). The real target is the divergence comment block at lines 85-90 plus the public method TryRedirectForManipulation at line 91, which still takes global::Windows.UI.Input.PointerPoint. The comment itself states: "For now, the API diverges from WinUI... In Uno 6, we will make a breaking change to match WinUI." So this is NOT dead-code removal but a deliberate, deferred WinUI-parity signature change. CurrentState=still-applies: divergence is unfixed in the working tree. To complete: flip the parameter type here and on ICompositionTarget.TryRedirectForManipulation (ICompositionTarget.cs:12) and its impl CompositionTarget.cs:78; the internal compositionTarget.TryRedirectForManipulation call at line 100 then no longer needs the implicit conversion. The implicit operators in PointerPoint.cs (lines 69 and 92) already cover both directions, so most call sites (ScrollPresenter, ScrollViewerIRefreshInfoProviderAdapter, samples, runtime test) compile unchanged because they pass GetCurrentPoint(null) which is already Microsoft.UI.Input.PointerPoint. The WinAppSDKSyncGenerator special-case (Generator.cs:1613) that force-skips this method may need review/removal once the signature matches WinUI. Not native-rendering-only, so not auto-safe; danger 2 (obscure public API, source-compatible for normal usage).

assessments.summary: Remove Uno's lenient acceptance of WPF/Silverlight-style clr-namespace: xmlns declarations from the XAML source generators and runtime XAML reader, leaving only WinUI's using: syntax.
assessments.customerImpact: Confirmed: WinUI accepts only `using:` for custom-type xmlns (Microsoft docs: the value "must contain the keyword using:"); clr-namespace: is a WPF/MAUI construct WinUI never supported. Uno currently accepts clr-namespace: as a WPF/Silverlight-migration convenience. Removing it breaks any app/library migrated from WPF or Silverlight that still has clr-namespace: xmlns (a documented Uno convenience in wpf-winui-equivalents.md and the Silverlight migration guides) with a hard XAML-compile error. In-repo it would immediately break 7 active SamplesApp XAML files (Windows_UI_Xaml_Media/ImageBrushTests/*.xaml) and two generator test fixtures (XamlGenerationTests/ImplicitConversion.xaml, CustomMarkupExtensions.xaml). Greenfield WinUI-style projects are unaffected. Likelihood a typical new app depends on it is low; likelihood a migrated codebase does is moderate.
assessments.decisionQuestion: The investigation is settled: clr-namespace: is NOT valid WinUI XAML under any scenario (WinUI mandates using:); Uno accepts it only as a WPF/Silverlight-migration leniency. Given that, do we actually remove this leniency for the major version (breaking migrated apps and requiring our own SamplesApp/test XAML + docs to switch to using:), or keep it as a documented non-WinUI compatibility affordance? If removing, do we hard-error or emit a deprecation diagnostic for one cycle first?
assessments.notes: Investigation answer is definitive (Microsoft Learn: WinUI custom-type xmlns "must contain the keyword using:"; WPF uses clr-namespace:, MAUI accepts both, WinUI only using:). This is purely compile-time/XAML-reader plumbing shared by ALL targets (Skia included) — it does NOT touch native-target rendering, so it is fully independent of the native-rendering drop (nativeOnly=false). Five code paths to clean if removed: XamlCodeGeneration.cs ~L732, XamlFileGenerator.Reflection.cs GetTrimmedNamespace ~L808, XamlCodeBehindParser.cs ~L138, XamlTypeResolver.cs SourceFindType ~L364, plus the vendored System.Xaml parser (PrefixLookup ~L80, XamlSchemaContext ~L407) — though the System.Xaml library is a general XAML implementation where clr-namespace: is spec-mandated and likely should be left intact. Note the recent 001-global-xaml-namespaces feature deliberately lists clr-namespace: as a resolution fallback (research.md L49), so this item should be reconciled with that design. Effort M because of the in-repo SamplesApp/test-fixture migration and docs updates that would accompany removal; danger 3 (advanced/migration scenario, not a mainstream API, but a real documented behavioral surface).

assessments.summary: Delete the manually-emitted `op_Explicit` binary-compat shim method on Microsoft.UI.Input.PointerPoint (kept only so old binaries that bound to the former explicit cast keep linking), leaving just the implicit conversion operators.
assessments.customerImpact: Pure binary-compat concern, not a source break. Uno's own internal callers (e.g. PointerRoutedEventArgs.cs:42's explicit cast) already resolve to the implicit operator and recompile fine; any app/library source that casts MUX PointerPoint to WUX PointerPoint also still compiles via the implicit operator. The ONLY thing that breaks is a third-party binary compiled against an older Uno that emitted an IL call to the literal `op_Explicit` method — it would get a MissingMethodException at runtime. That symbol exists solely to dodge a binary break introduced during the InteractionTracker change; depending on it directly is extremely rare. Practically near-zero real-world impact, and binary compat is expected to break at a major version anyway.
assessments.decisionQuestion: This method exists only to preserve binary compatibility (the explicit-to-implicit operator change is a binary break). Since this is a major version, is intentionally breaking that binary compat acceptable, allowing the shim to be removed?
assessments.notes: Located at src/Uno.UI/UI/Input/WinRT/PointerPoint.cs lines 60-67. The method is guarded by `#if IS_UNO_UI_PROJECT`, so it only exists in the Uno.UI generic (Skia/cross-runtime) assembly under the Microsoft.UI.Input namespace; it is input plumbing, NOT native-target rendering code, so nativeOnly=false. The in-code comment (lines 60-64) documents exactly why it was added: C# CS0557 forbids declaring both implicit and explicit conversions between the same two types, and switching the historical `op_Explicit` to `implicit operator` is a binary (not source) breaking change, so the raw `op_Explicit` method is hand-emitted alongside the implicit operator. It carries `[EditorBrowsable(EditorBrowsableState.Never)]`. Removal is a one-line-block delete with no Uno-internal callers (only the generic operator check in Uno.ReferenceImplComparer/Program.cs:145 references the name, and that is a string match that needs no change). currentState=still-applies: the shim is present in the tree (recent commits to this file were unrelated #if/cleanup churn). Effort S; trivial edit, but flagged needsDecision because removal is by definition the binary break the shim was created to prevent, so a maintainer should confirm the major-version break is intended.

assessments.summary: Remove the public Uno-only generic class Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Setter<T> (and its ICSharpPropertySetter marker interface), plus the backward-compat shims it forced onto SetterBase (set_Property, OnStringPropertyChanged) and the extra DependencyObject parameter on SetterBase.TryGetSetterValue.
assessments.customerImpact: Setter<T> is a legacy Uno-specific (NOT WinUI) generic setter for code-based styling via Action<T>. It is public, so a customer could have written `new Setter<MyControl>("Prop", c => ...)` in a code-defined Style; those call sites would fail to compile. Likelihood is very low: it is not part of the WinUI API surface, has zero instantiations anywhere in this repo (no samples, no tests, no framework code create it), is undocumented in modern guidance, and the standard XAML/Setter path does not use it. SetterBase.cs already carries an explicit `// ... can be removed in Uno 6 once we remove Setter<T>` note, confirming it is a planned legacy cleanup.
assessments.notes: Setter<T> lives in src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Setter.Generic.cs as `public class Setter<T> : SetterBase, ICSharpPropertySetter`. It is distinct from the WinUI `sealed partial class Setter` in Setter.cs. Repo-wide grep finds only three references to it: its own definition, and Style.cs:295 which consumes the `ICSharpPropertySetter` marker interface (`else if (setter is ICSharpPropertySetter propertySetter) map[propertySetter.Property] = setter;`). There are ZERO `new Setter<...>` instantiations anywhere (framework, SamplesApp, RuntimeTests, UnitTests, docs). Removal cascade: (1) delete Setter.Generic.cs incl. ICSharpPropertySetter; (2) drop the `else if (setter is ICSharpPropertySetter ...)` branch in Style.EnumerateSetters; (3) per the explicit comment at SetterBase.cs:18-19, drop the now-unneeded `DependencyObject @do` parameter from SetterBase.TryGetSetterValue and Setter.TryGetSetterValue; (4) remove the binary-compat shims `set_Property`/`OnStringPropertyChanged` on SetterBase (lines 21-28) that exist solely for Setter<T>. Platform-agnostic core Uno.UI code, no native-rendering dependency, so not nativeOnly. Danger 2 (not 1) only because it is technically public surface, but it is obscure legacy with no realistic consumers. No GitHub reference provided; assessment is from code. Change still fully applies — nothing has been removed yet.

assessments.summary: Remove the public opt-in compatibility flag Uno.UI.FeatureConfiguration.Control.UseLegacyContentAlignment and hard-code Center/Center as the default Horizontal/VerticalContentAlignment (matching WinUI), dropping the legacy Left/Top fallback.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only apps that explicitly set FeatureConfiguration.Control.UseLegacyContentAlignment = true to keep pre-parity Left/Top content alignment defaults. That is an obscure, opt-in legacy flag (default false, never set by templates, no docs/samples/tests reference it); a typical app/library never touches it. Apps that did set it would see content shift to Center/Center and get a compile error on the removed symbol. Easy fix: set HorizontalContentAlignment/VerticalContentAlignment explicitly where needed.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should the legacy opt-in flag UseLegacyContentAlignment be removed entirely (hard-coding the WinUI-correct Center/Center default), or kept as a no-op/obsolete shim for one more cycle to avoid a hard compile break for the few apps that set it?
assessments.notes: Confirmed exactly 3 references in repo: declaration at src/Uno.UI/FeatureConfiguration.cs:137 (public static bool, default false) and two usages at src/SourceGenerators/.../DependencyPropertyMixinGenerator.cs:208 and :211 where it picks Left/Top vs Center/Center defaults for Control.HorizontalContentAlignment/VerticalContentAlignment (both AffectsArrange). No tests, samples, docs, or .tt files reference it (the old DependencyPropertyMixins.tt is gone; the generator is now the C# DependencyPropertyMixinGenerator). Removal is mechanical: delete the property and replace the two ternaries with the literal Center/Center values. Item is phrased as "Consider removing" -> needsDecision. Not native-only: lives in Uno.UI core + the cross-target source generator, so it affects Skia too.

assessments.summary: Remove the orphaned public static feature flag FeatureConfiguration.UIElement.ShowClippingBounds, which is declared but read nowhere in the codebase.
assessments.customerImpact: Source/binary break only for the rare app that explicitly sets FeatureConfiguration.UIElement.ShowClippingBounds. It is an obscure iOS-native-only diagnostic visualization flag with zero remaining consumers in the codebase, so setting it already does nothing. Virtually no production app or library depends on it; impact is negligible.
assessments.notes: Confirmed: single declaration at src/Uno.UI/FeatureConfiguration.cs:736 inside the public static nested class FeatureConfiguration.UIElement: `public static bool ShowClippingBounds { get; set; }`. A repo-wide grep for ShowClippingBounds returns ONLY this declaration line - no readers. A case-insensitive grep for "ClippingBounds" finds only unrelated local variables in VisualTreeHelper.cs hit-testing/trace code. The XML doc states "This feature is only supported on iOS, for now," i.e. it was a native-iOS-only diagnostic that no longer has any wired-up consumer; it is dead public surface. It is NOT nativeOnly in the auto-safe rendering-removal sense because it lives in the cross-platform Uno.UI FeatureConfiguration (public API surface), not in native iOS rendering code being deleted - hence judged on normal merit. Danger 2 (not 1) because it is genuinely public settable API; danger is low because it is an obscure diagnostic flag with no effect. Effort S: delete the property + its doc comment (4 lines). No dependencies.

assessments.summary: Remove the deprecated public Foundation.NSObjectExtensions.ValidateDispose extension method (UIKit-only), plus its FeatureConfiguration.UIElement.FailOnNSObjectExtensionsValidateDispose flag and the feature-flags.md doc section.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only legacy native iOS/tvOS/MacCatalyst (UIKit) apps that still call NSObjectExtensions.ValidateDispose in a custom NSObject/UIView Dispose override. The method was already loudly deprecated ("DO NOT USE IT"), has zero internal callers, exists only on the native UIKit rendering target (which is being dropped), and never compiled for Skia. A typical app/library almost certainly never references it; only very old hand-written native iOS view-disposal code would.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in this worktree: method defined at NSObjectExtensions.UIKit.cs:23 (public, namespace Foundation), guarded by FeatureConfiguration.UIElement.FailOnNSObjectExtensionsValidateDispose (FeatureConfiguration.cs:782, no #if guard but only meaningful on UIKit). Grep for '.ValidateDispose(' finds no callers anywhere in the repo. Already marked "THIS METHOD IS DEPRECATED ... removed in the next major version" in its doc-comment and documented as deprecated in doc/articles/feature-flags.md:154-158. The .UIKit.cs suffix means it compiles only for native iOS/tvOS/MacCatalyst, i.e. native rendering targets being dropped. Removal is a 3-spot delete: the extension class/method file, the feature-config property, and the doc section. Note the FailOn... property currently lacks an #if so it's technically visible on all platforms' FeatureConfiguration surface; delete it alongside the method. Public-API removal is in principle a breaking change, hence not auto-zero, but danger=1 per rubric: native-target-only + already-deprecated dead code with no callers.

assessments.summary: Change Uno.Collections.MaterializableList<T> from a public class to internal, since it is an internal UI-thread visual-tree helper that no public API exposes.
assessments.customerImpact: Binary/source-breaking only for code that directly references the type name Uno.Collections.MaterializableList<T> (e.g. a custom panel/control author who grabbed the concrete children collection or called the type's ctors). Extremely unlikely in a typical app or library: it is an undocumented internal helper, every in-repo consumer reaches it via internal members (UIElement.GetChildren, VisualTreeHelper.GetChildren/GetManagedVisualChildren*, BindableView/BindableUIView private fields), and the public VisualTreeHelper.GetChildren surface returns IEnumerable<DependencyObject>, not this type. No public signature changes are needed.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should MaterializableList<T> be demoted from public to internal now (clean major-version visibility tightening), or kept public for the rare advanced consumer who may reference the concrete visual-tree children collection directly?
assessments.notes: Confirmed still public: line 19 of src/Uno.UWP/Collections/MaterializableList.cs reads `public class MaterializableList<T> : IList<T>, IReadOnlyList<T>` in the public Uno.Collections namespace (Uno.UWP assembly). It is tracked as public API surface in build/PackageDiffIgnore.xml (line 1659, an existing entry covering a prior GetEnumerator return-type change). All ctors and most members are public, but a couple of helpers (GetReverseSortedEnumerator, ClearCachedReverseSortedList) are already internal. Every consumer found in the repo uses it through internal members or private fields only; grep found no public member that returns or accepts MaterializableList<T>, so making the type internal is a self-contained edit (just flip `public`->`internal` and the public ctors/members can stay or be tidied). Used by both native (BindableView.Android.cs, BindableUIView.UIKit.cs) and Skia/cross-runtime paths (UIElement.crossruntime/.skia/.wasm, VisualTreeHelper), so it is NOT native-only and survives the native-rendering drop. After flipping, the PackageDiffIgnore entry for the type can be removed/updated since the member would no longer be public. Verified by inspection only (no build run).

assessments.summary: Delete the empty no-op RestoreBindings()/ClearBindings() methods from DependencyObjectStore.Binder.cs and stop emitting their public wrappers from DependencyObjectGenerator (also drop the native-iOS SyncBinder call to ClearBindings).
assessments.customerImpact: The store-level methods are internal-surface; the customer-visible part is the public ClearBindings()/RestoreBindings() pair the DependencyObjectGenerator emits on every DependencyObject (i.e. effectively every control). Both are empty no-ops, explicitly commented "Obsolete method kept for binary compatibility", and marked [EditorBrowsable(Never)] so they don't even surface in IntelliSense. A typical app/library almost never calls them; removing them is a source-break only for the rare consumer that explicitly invoked these do-nothing methods, and an ABI break for precompiled binaries. Low realistic blast radius for a major version.
assessments.notes:
Verified still-applies: both methods are present and have empty bodies (DependencyObjectStore.Binder.cs lines 198 and 213). Issue #13046 is OPEN, labeled kind/breaking-change + good-first-issue; Martin commented "Still relevant" recently and it was assigned to a contributor. A 5.0 attempt was declined by jeromelaban for timing only, not correctness ("Your change is correct"), so this is genuinely unfinished, not already-done.

Scope of the removal (4 touch points):
1. DependencyObjectStore.Binder.cs — delete the two empty methods + their XML doc.
2. DependencyObjectGenerator.cs (lines ~486-504) — stop emitting the public ClearBindings()/RestoreBindings() wrappers it puts on every DependencyObject (commented "Obsolete method kept for binary compatibility", [EditorBrowsable(Never)]).
3. FrameworkElementUIKitMixinGenerator.cs line 390 — native-iOS SyncBinder() calls ClearBindings() when leaving the visual tree; that call must be removed (or it'll reference a deleted method). This is native-UIKit rendering code being dropped anyway, so harmless either way.
4. Test golden Given_DependencyObjectGenerator.cs (lines 172-184) expects the generated wrappers — must regenerate/update the expected source or the generator test fails.
5. build/PackageDiffIgnore.xml lines 996-997 reference a *different* symbol — Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.ElementFactory.ClearBindings/RestoreBindings (WinAppSDK API-diff ignores), unrelated to the DependencyObject methods; leave those alone.

Danger is 2 not 1 because there IS a real (if obscure, EditorBrowsable-hidden) public API surface emitted on every DependencyObject, so it's a true public-API removal rather than purely internal dead code. Not nativeOnly: the core methods and the generated public wrappers are cross-platform (Skia included); only the SyncBinder caller is native-iOS. No dependency on the DependencyObject-as-class item — these are instance methods on the generated store provider regardless of whether DependencyObject is a class or interface.

assessments.summary: Flip the default of Uno.WinRTFeatureConfiguration.ApplicationLanguages.UseLegacyPrimaryLanguageOverride from true to false, so setting PrimaryLanguageOverride no longer immediately changes CultureInfo.CurrentCulture during the setter (matching WinUI's restart-to-apply behavior).
assessments.customerImpact: Apps that switch UI language at runtime by setting Windows.Globalization.ApplicationLanguages.PrimaryLanguageOverride and rely on Uno's non-WinUI behavior of immediately swapping CultureInfo.CurrentCulture/CurrentUICulture mid-session would stop seeing the culture change take effect without an app restart. This is a common Uno scenario (runtime localization without restart), but the break is a default-only flip with a trivial one-line opt-out (set UseLegacyPrimaryLanguageOverride = true again). Apps that don't touch PrimaryLanguageOverride at runtime, or that already restart on language change, are unaffected. The flag is the gate around the ApplyCulture() call in the PrimaryLanguageOverride setter.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Issue #13704 explicitly asks the maintainers "do you agree?" about taking this break — do we flip UseLegacyPrimaryLanguageOverride's default to false now (aligning with WinUI's restart-to-apply semantics) and accept that runtime-language-switching apps must opt back in or restart, or keep legacy immediate-culture-apply as the Uno default?
assessments.notes: The change is a one-character default flip at src/Uno.UWP/FeatureConfiguration/WinRTFeatureConfiguration.cs:22 (`= true` -> `= false`). Default is still `true` in the current tree, so the change STILL APPLIES (not already-done). Behavioral mechanics confirmed by reading the code: the flag only gates the extra `ApplyCulture()` call inside the `PrimaryLanguageOverride` setter (ApplicationLanguages.cs:45-48); `ApplyCulture()` is still always invoked once at app startup from Application's constructor (Application.cs:122), and `ApplyLanguages()` (which updates the Languages list and resource resolution) runs unconditionally regardless of the flag. So with the default false, only the immediate mid-session CultureInfo swap is lost. The flag is genuine public surface (Uno.WinRTFeatureConfiguration.ApplicationLanguages), not internal-only, which lifts it above danger 1-2. No migration doc or breaking-changes entry references it yet, and there are no test references to the flag. Issue #13704 is OPEN, starter difficulty, labelled kind/breaking-change, and is phrased as a question to maintainers — hence needsDecision.

assessments.summary: Fill in the currently-empty else-branch in XamlFileGenerator.RewriteUri so relative System.Uri / ImageSource string literals on non-framework-type owners are rewritten to ms-resource:/// (WinUI-aligned) instead of being emitted verbatim.
assessments.customerImpact: Affects XAML where a relative URI string is set on a property of a type that is NOT Image/ImageSource (e.g. a custom control with a Uri property, or BitmapImage/MediaSource-style relative URIs bound through non-Image owners). Today Uno emits the raw relative string; the change would prefix/convert it to ms-resource:/// to match WinUI. Most apps put relative image URIs on Image/Source (already special-cased and unchanged), so the typical app is unaffected; advanced apps that set relative URIs on custom/non-framework Uri properties could see the compiled value change from a bare relative string to an ms-resource:/// URI, which is breaking if they relied on the current passthrough.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should non-framework-owner relative URIs be rewritten to ms-resource:///Files/-prefixed values (matching XamlFilePathHelper.LocalResourcePrefix / WinUI resource-loader semantics), and is this conversion acceptable to ship as a breaking change in this major, or should it stay opt-in to avoid changing the compiled value for apps relying on the current verbatim passthrough?
assessments.notes: Located at src/SourceGenerators/.../XamlFileGenerator.cs lines ~5300-5326 (local function RewriteUri inside the member-value builder). The else branch (lines ~5319-5322) is an empty placeholder containing only: `// Breaking change, support for ms-resource:// for non framework owners (https://github.com/unoplatform/uno/issues/8339)`. RewriteUri is called from two sites: case "System.Uri" (line 5136) and case "Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Media.ImageSource" (line 5198). Image/ImageSource owners are already handled (ms-appx:/// + base-uri prefix); the gap is only the non-framework-owner path, which currently returns `@"{rawValue}"` verbatim. Runtime side already has the building blocks: XamlFilePathHelper.MSResourceIdentifier = "ms-resource:///" and LocalResourcePrefix = "ms-resource:///Files/". This is platform-agnostic source-generator codegen (NOT native-rendering code), so it is NOT auto-safe under the native-drop; it survives into Skia. Work is not done — the else branch is genuinely empty at HEAD c9a201821e. Effort M because it needs the codegen plus golden-output updates in Uno.UI.SourceGenerators.Tests and a runtime test confirming the resolved URI matches WinUI. Confidence: code-review/inspection only (no build run).

assessments.summary: Reparent Uno's RadioMenuFlyoutItem from ToggleMenuFlyoutItem to MenuFlyoutItem to match WinUI's public hierarchy, porting the toggle behavior it currently inherits, and remove the RadioMenuFlyoutItem entry from _skipBaseTypes in the UWP SyncGenerator so it emits the correct base type.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks any consumer that relies on RadioMenuFlyoutItem currently being a ToggleMenuFlyoutItem: `is/as ToggleMenuFlyoutItem` checks, casts, ToggleMenuFlyoutItem-typed fields/collections, and implicit styles targeting ToggleMenuFlyoutItem that today also apply to radio items. This is the OPPOSITE of WinUI (where RadioMenuFlyoutItem only derives from MenuFlyoutItem), so the change actually moves toward parity and would un-break parity-sensitive code, but breaks the (uncommon) Uno-specific dependency on the extra base level. The Uno-only `new IsChecked`/`new IsCheckedProperty` shadowing exists solely because of the ToggleMenuFlyoutItem base and would likely be removed too, a subtle change for anyone binding the shadowed DP. Typical apps use RadioMenuFlyoutItem directly and are unaffected; only advanced code keyed on the ToggleMenuFlyoutItem relationship breaks.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Is the WinUI-parity benefit of reparenting RadioMenuFlyoutItem to MenuFlyoutItem (and duplicating the toggle behavior it now inherits from ToggleMenuFlyoutItem) worth the implementation cost and the small break for code that relies on the current ToggleMenuFlyoutItem base, or should the _skipBaseTypes workaround simply remain?
assessments.notes: Verified in this repo: RadioMenuFlyoutItem.cs line 15 still declares `: ToggleMenuFlyoutItem`; the class comment (lines 13-14) explains the deliberate choice. The SyncGenerator workaround is still present at Generator.cs lines 111-113 in the `_skipBaseTypes` array, used by HasValidBaseType (line 1112-1114) to suppress emitting a mismatched base type. WinUI source confirms the intended target: D:/Work/microsoft-ui-xaml2/controls/dev/RadioMenuFlyoutItem/RadioMenuFlyoutItem.idl declares `unsealed runtimeclass RadioMenuFlyoutItem : Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.MenuFlyoutItem` (single inheritance, NOT double). The in-code comment claiming WinUI has "double inheritance / secretly derives from ToggleMenuFlyoutItem" is the historical justification but does not match current WinUI 3 public surface, so the SyncGenerator note's premise ("if WinUI 3 removed the double inheritance") is slightly off. Effort is M because removing the ToggleMenuFlyoutItem base requires re-implementing/porting the toggle/IsChecked plumbing currently inherited (note the `new IsChecked`/`new IsCheckedProperty` shadowing that exists only to coexist with the inherited DP), updating the Generated stub regeneration, and possibly PackageDiffIgnore.xml. Not native-rendering-only: lives in shared Uno.UI (netX/Skia) cross-platform control code, so the native-target drop does not auto-make it safe. No GitHub reference found; assessed purely from repo code.

assessments.summary: Rename the Uno.UI.Toolkit assembly and root namespace (and its XAML xmlns "using:Uno.UI.Toolkit") to a new name to avoid confusion with the separate Uno Toolkit product.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks any app or library that references the publicly-shipped Uno.UI.Toolkit assembly (shipped inside the Uno.WinUI NuGet package). Both C# consumers ("using Uno.UI.Toolkit;" for ElevatedView, VisibleBoundsPadding, CommandBarExtensions/back-button handling, AutomationPropertiesExtensions, UIElementExtensions, FromJsonExtension, etc.) and XAML consumers (xmlns:toolkit="using:Uno.UI.Toolkit") would need to update. VisibleBoundsPadding and CommandBarExtensions (BackButton/navigation command) in particular are commonly used in real apps, so a meaningful share of mobile/desktop apps depend on this. Renaming the assembly also breaks any direct assembly reference and any fully-qualified type usage.
assessments.decisionQuestion: What is the new assembly/namespace name, and will we ship type-forwarders + a XAML-namespace alias (and an obsoletion period) so existing C#/XAML "Uno.UI.Toolkit" references keep working, or is this a hard rename with no shims for the major version?
assessments.notes: No rename has happened yet: assembly name, RootNamespace, and "namespace Uno.UI.Toolkit" are all still Uno.UI.Toolkit across 18 public types (ElevatedView, MapResources, MenuFlyoutExtensions, DiagnosticsOverlay, FromJsonExtension, plus many static extension classes) and ~164 referencing files in-repo. The assembly is publicly shipped via build/nuget/Uno.WinUI.nuspec, so this is real customer-facing surface, not internal. The public XAML contract "using:Uno.UI.Toolkit" makes this worse than a pure C# rename because XAML has no implicit type-forwarding. Danger 4 (commonly-used public API rename, not pervasive type-system change). The issue (#12322) is a "starter"/breaking enhancement and leaves the target name and compat strategy unspecified -> needs a design decision. Effort M: mechanical rename is small, but updating the 5 csproj variants, _AdjustedOutputProjects entries, nuspec, Generic.xaml, the VisibleBoundsPadding.cs link shared from Uno.UI, source-generator XAML xmlns handling, and providing back-compat shims adds up. Not nativeOnly: spans all targets incl. Skia and is non-rendering library surface.

assessments.summary: Make FlyoutBase.DataContext non-public (it is absent on WinUI's FlyoutBase) — really a symptom of Uno emitting a public DataContext DP on every DependencyObject instead of only on FrameworkElement.
assessments.customerImpact: In WinUI, FlyoutBase (a DependencyObject, not a FrameworkElement) has no DataContext at all; Uno exposes a public DataContext + DataContextProperty on it (and on every non-FE DependencyObject). Apps that read/set flyout.DataContext, bind to it, or rely on the inherited public surface would break if it were removed. More importantly the root fix (restrict the generated DataContext to FrameworkElement) touches Uno's DataContext-inheritance model that propagates through the whole DependencyObject store tree — FlyoutBase itself forwards its inherited DataContext to its Popup/presenter (FlyoutBase.cs OnDataContextChangedPartial + ShowAttachedFlyout), so naively removing it risks breaking flyout/menuflyout binding for many apps. A FlyoutBase-only shadow is awkward because the member is inherited from the generator, not declared on FlyoutBase.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Do we narrow the public DataContext/DataContextProperty emission to FrameworkElement-derived types only (the umbrella fix #13201), accepting the risk to Uno's DependencyObject DataContext-inheritance chain — and if so, how do non-FrameworkElement DOs like FlyoutBase that today forward inherited DataContext to their Popup keep working (internal DataContext kept, only public surface hidden)?
assessments.notes: Confirmed in code: DataContext and DataContextProperty are NOT declared in any FlyoutBase file — they are auto-generated as `public object DataContext` + `public static DependencyProperty DataContextProperty` by DependencyObjectGenerator.WriteBinderImplementation (lines ~619-641), applied to every root DependencyObject (FlyoutBase : DependencyObject, FlyoutBase.cs:36). So BC54 is not a Flyout-specific edit; it is one visible instance of the broader divergence. The referenced issue #12491 is CLOSED as a duplicate of the still-OPEN umbrella issue #13201 ("DataContext should only be available on FrameworkElement"), whose body names DependencyObjectGenerator as the cause. The undesired public DataContext is still present on FlyoutBase today (verified no shadowing/EditorBrowsable hiding exists in the Flyout files). Danger 4 (not 5) because it is a visibility/surface reduction on a real but not pervasive set of public members; bumped above 3 because the only clean fix is foundational in the generator and the DataContext inheritance flow is load-bearing for flyout binding. Note FlyoutBase relies on this inherited DataContext functionally (OnDataContextChangedPartial forwards to Popup; ShowAttachedFlyout sets FlyoutBase.DataContextProperty from the owner) — any fix must keep that working internally while only hiding the public surface.

assessments.summary: Replace the deprecated Android.Preferences.PreferenceManager.GetDefaultSharedPreferences call in ApplicationDataContainer.Android.cs with the AndroidX.Preference equivalent (or Context.GetSharedPreferences with an explicit name).
assessments.customerImpact: No public API surface changes — CreatePreferences() is a private internal detail of ApplicationDataContainer's SharedPreferences-backed PropertySet, so no caller breaks at compile time. The genuine risk is data continuity: Android.Preferences.PreferenceManager.GetDefaultSharedPreferences uses the legacy default prefs file ("<package>_preferences"), and swapping to AndroidX.Preference.PreferenceManager or a differently-named GetSharedPreferences store would point at a different backing file, silently orphaning every existing Android user's persisted ApplicationData.LocalSettings/RoamingSettings on upgrade. Any Uno Android app that reads/writes ApplicationDataContainer settings is affected if the storage name changes; a like-for-like swap that preserves the exact prefs name has effectively zero customer impact.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should we migrate to AndroidX.Preference.PreferenceManager (adding the AndroidX Preference dependency) or simply call Context.GetSharedPreferences with the legacy default name, and either way how do we guarantee the new code reads the SAME backing file as the old default so existing users' stored settings are not orphaned on app upgrade?
assessments.notes: Confirmed only one usage in the repo: ApplicationDataContainer.Android.cs:29, `PreferenceManager.GetDefaultSharedPreferences(ApplicationData.GetAndroidAppContext())` via `using Android.Preferences;` (line 8). Issue #1833 is still OPEN and the code still uses the deprecated API, so the work still applies. This lives in Uno.UWP (platform non-UI WinRT API), which the migration spec explicitly keeps actively maintained and which Skia-on-Android still consumes — so it is NOT auto-safe native rendering code (nativeOnly=false), but it is also not a public-surface breaking change. Danger is low (private impl, no API change); the only real hazard is picking a replacement that changes the SharedPreferences backing-file name and orphaning persisted settings. The issue body itself raises the migration question ("how should the contents be migrated from PreferenceManager to PreferenceLibrary on upgrade"), which is why this needs a decision. Simplest low-risk fix: replace with `ApplicationData.GetAndroidAppContext().GetSharedPreferences(context.PackageName + "_preferences", FileCreationMode.Private)` to keep the exact legacy file, avoiding a new dependency. Other ApplicationDataContainer partials (.Apple/.skia/.wasm/.cs) are unaffected.

assessments.summary: Remove the Android availability of the WinRTFeatureConfiguration.Focus.EnableExperimentalKeyboardFocus opt-in flag so keyboard focus is unconditionally on for Android — but the flag is already gated to iOS/tvOS only, so the Android removal is done.
assessments.customerImpact: Negligible. The flag is an opt-in experimental setting (default false) and on Android it no longer exists in the public surface at all (property is compiled only under #if __IOS__ || __TVOS__). An Android app that previously set this flag would already fail to compile in the current codebase, so any such code has already been migrated. Keyboard focus is already always-on for Android. The flag remains valid and functional for iOS/tvOS, which is intentional and unchanged.
assessments.notes: The Android-specific removal this item describes is already implemented. In WinRTFeatureConfiguration.Focus.cs the property is gated `#if __IOS__ || __TVOS__` (line 7) and its XML doc already says "keyboard focus is now always enabled on Android." git blame: commit 44cf9a8629 ("chore: Adjust all iOS specific files in UWP lib") flipped the gate from `#if __IOS__` to `#if __IOS__ || __TVOS__`, completing the Android-TV-related adjustment referenced by this item. All remaining consumers are Apple-only: NativeWindow.UIKit.cs:88 (UIKit-suffixed) and App.xaml.cs:587 (under `#if __APPLE_UIKIT__`). No Android-target code references the flag, so removing the Android availability is complete and safe (danger 1). NOTE: the flag is NOT being removed for iOS/tvOS — it is still a valid experimental opt-in there (docs focus-management.md line 27-35), so fully deleting the flag is a different, larger change and is out of scope for BC56. nativeOnly=false because the symbol lives in Uno.UWP's WinRTFeatureConfiguration (a platform config API), not native rendering code being dropped — but it is effectively a no-op now. Minor housekeeping only: there is a typo "keybopard" in the XML comment that could be cleaned up, and the focus-management.md note still says the switch-to-default-on is "future" which is now true for Android — optional doc polish, not required by this item.

assessments.summary: Change the public `OnTemplateRecycled()` method on TextBox, ToggleSwitch, ToggleButton (and native ScrollContentPresenter) to explicit `IFrameworkTemplatePoolAware` interface implementation so it disappears from the public surface.
assessments.customerImpact: Only breaks code that explicitly calls `textBox.OnTemplateRecycled()` / `.OnTemplateRecycled()` on ToggleSwitch/ToggleButton. This is a `[UnoOnly]` internal-interface method that never existed in WinUI/UWP, so no portable app code relies on it; a typical app/library has essentially zero reason to call it (it is invoked internally by FrameworkTemplatePool during recycling). One in-repo caller exists: the runtime test Given_TextBox.cs:225, which must be updated to cast to the interface. External impact is negligible.
assessments.notes: Partially already done: ContentPresenter.cs:1030 and ScrollViewer.cs:1040 ALREADY use explicit `void IFrameworkTemplatePoolAware.OnTemplateRecycled()`. The 4 remaining public implementations are TextBox.cs:1399, ToggleSwitch.cs:204, ToggleButton.cs:90, and ScrollContentPresenter.Native.cs:16 (native-only, gated `#if __APPLE_UIKIT__ || __ANDROID__`). The interface IFrameworkTemplatePoolAware is already `internal [UnoOnly]`, so explicit implementation fully removes the member from the public API. The framework's own call site FrameworkTemplatePool.cs:498 already invokes via the interface (`templateAwareElement.OnTemplateRecycled()`), so it is unaffected. In-repo fixes needed: Given_TextBox.cs:225 calls it publicly and must cast `((IFrameworkTemplatePoolAware)textBox).OnTemplateRecycled()`; test stubs ReuseCountGrid.cs:17 and the unit-test stub in Given_FrameworkTemplate.cs:282 declare `public void OnTemplateRecycled()` and could keep public or also go explicit (cosmetic). The ScrollContentPresenter.Native part touches native-only code that is being dropped, but TextBox/ToggleSwitch/ToggleButton are core cross-platform Skia controls, so this is not native-only overall. Labelled difficulty/starter on the issue; trivial mechanical change. Evidence: code review only (grep + reads), no build run.

assessments.summary: Stop emitting the public DataContext property (plus DataContextProperty, DataContextChanged event, and OnDataContextChanged) from DependencyObjectGenerator for every DependencyObject; restrict it to FrameworkElement only, matching WinUI.
assessments.customerImpact: Today Uno exposes a public `DataContext` property, a `DataContextProperty` DependencyProperty, and a `DataContextChanged` event on EVERY DependencyObject (Brush, Transform, Setter, DependencyObjectCollection, FlyoutBase, GradientStop, etc.) — far more than WinUI, where these live only on FrameworkElement. Moving them to FrameworkElement-only would be a source/binary-breaking removal for any app or library that: reads/sets `.DataContext` on a non-FrameworkElement DO; binds a Brush/Transform/Setter to a DataContext; subscribes to `DataContextChanged` on such a type; or references `Brush.DataContextProperty`. XAML that relies on a Brush or other non-FE inheriting the ambient DataContext for its bindings (a common pattern in Uno that WinUI does NOT support) would silently stop resolving. Likelihood a typical app trips this: moderate-to-high for libraries doing dynamic styling/binding on brushes; lower for simple apps. Generated-output change also forces x:Bind/XAML golden regeneration.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Do we move DataContext/DataContextProperty/DataContextChanged/OnDataContextChanged to FrameworkElement-only now (WinUI parity, but breaks Uno's de-facto "bind a Brush to DataContext" pattern), or keep emitting them on all DependencyObjects for back-compat? If we move it, where does the DataContext DP get registered/owned (FrameworkElement directly vs. still on the generated binder gated to FE-derived types), and how is inheritance/propagation re-wired in DependencyObjectStore which currently assumes every DO carries a DataContextProperty?
assessments.notes: Confirmed root cause: DependencyObjectGenerator.WriteBinderImplementation (DependencyObjectGenerator.cs lines ~619-649) unconditionally emits `public object DataContext`, `public static DependencyProperty DataContextProperty`, the `DataContextChanged` event (WriteInitializer, line ~474), and `OnDataContextChanged`/`OnDataContextChangedPartial` for the root DependencyObject implementer of EVERY inheritance chain — not just FrameworkElement. The only FrameworkElement-awareness present is choosing the DataContextChanged sender argument (`this` / `this as FrameworkElement` / `null`, lines 600-617), which actually proves the generator KNOWS many emitters are non-FE yet still emits the property. So the change still applies; it is NOT already done. Non-trivial because DependencyObjectStore wiring (DataContextStore.Binder.cs: `_dataContextProperty`, `__storeBackingField = new DependencyObjectStore(this, DataContextProperty)` at gen line ~769, inheritance propagation, FrameworkPropertyMetadataOptions.Inherits) assumes every store has a DataContextProperty; restricting emission means the store ctor and inheritance machinery must handle DOs with no DataContext DP. Effort M (generator + store plumbing + golden/goldens + package-diff allowlist + unit/runtime tests that assert DataContext on non-FE types must be removed/adjusted). Danger 4 not 5: it's a removal of an over-broad surface rather than a pervasive type-system change, but it touches a very commonly-bound member and a widely-used generated API. Issue #13201 is OPEN and is a low-detail bug report ("It should only be for FrameworkElement") with no repro — the design/migration story is genuinely ambiguous, hence needsDecision. No dependency on BC26 (DependencyObject-as-class): this is orthogonal — it concerns which DO subtypes get the property, not whether DO is a class vs interface.

assessments.summary: Flip nullable reference types on by default for the whole framework (project-level <Nullable>enable</Nullable> in src/Directory.Build.props) instead of the current per-file #nullable enable opt-in.
assessments.customerImpact: No runtime or binary break for consumers: NRT annotations are erased at runtime and only emitted as [Nullable]/[NullableContext] metadata, so a downstream app/library keeps compiling and running. The only externally visible effect is that more public API members would carry nullable annotations, which at most surfaces additional compiler warnings (never errors) in consumer code that has nullable enabled. The "breaking" weight is entirely internal: it is a build-config + source-quality change to the Uno framework, not a public-surface contract change.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Do we globally flip <Nullable>enable</Nullable> in src/Directory.Build.props now (forcing an all-at-once annotation pass over ~4300 not-yet-annotated files under TreatWarningsAsErrors), or keep the per-file opt-in convention and only enable nullable project-wide on a per-assembly basis as each assembly is fully annotated?
assessments.notes:
Confirmed current state in this repo: src/Directory.Build.props sets no <Nullable>; core assemblies Uno.UI, Uno.UWP, Uno.Foundation, Uno.UI.Composition do NOT set <Nullable>enable</Nullable> at project level. Nullable is opt-in per file via `#nullable enable` — only 308 of 4665 .cs files in Uno.UI (~6.6%) have it. The current convention is documented in .claude/rules/code-style.md line 23: "#nullable enable is per-file... large legacy files often don't — don't bulk-flip them." So the desired end-state (nullable on by default) is NOT yet done for the framework.

Already-done elsewhere (not what #9964 targets): newer/peripheral projects already set <Nullable>enable</Nullable> per-project — DevServer/RemoteControl (Uno.UI.DevServer.Cli, Uno.UI.RemoteControl.*), Uno.Sdk, Uno.HotReload, AddIns (Uno.WinUI.Graphics2DSK/3DGL, Uno.UI.Svg, MediaPlayer.Skia.X11, WebView.Skia.X11), and the consumer-facing SolutionTemplate Directory.Build.props (those are app templates, not framework source). So this is a partially-converged situation; the big core assemblies remain on per-file opt-in.

Danger=1 rationale: this is internal build-config / source-quality work (legacy/dead-code-style bucket of the rubric — internal-only, no public surface contract change). Not nativeOnly: it touches the cross-platform core (Skia path) too. Effort=L: with TreatWarningsAsErrors=true (Directory.Build.props line 243) a blanket flip would turn thousands of CS86xx nullability warnings into hard build errors across ~4300 files; realistically done assembly-by-assembly, hence the decision question. Recommend NOT a blanket Directory.Build.props flip in this PR; do it incrementally per assembly as annotation completes, which is also why this is flagged needsDecision rather than a mechanical change.

assessments.summary: Remove the Android-only native-popup rendering path: the PopupWindow/PopupMenu-backed code in Popup.Android.cs, NativePopup.Android.cs, NativePopupAdapter, NativePopupBase (Popup.Legacy.cs), the #if __ANDROID__ branches in Popup.WithPopupRoot.cs / MenuFlyout.Android.cs / ContentDialog.Visuals.cs, and the FeatureConfiguration.Popup.UseNativePopup flag.
assessments.customerImpact: Only affects native-Android apps that opted into native popups via FeatureConfiguration.Popup.UseNativePopup (default false) or instantiated the Android-only NativePopup public class. Both are Android-only and niche; the native Android Views target itself is being dropped, so any app still on it is already migrating to Skia where popups render via the managed PopupRoot. Skia-on-Android is unaffected (it never used this path). Realistically near-zero customers depend on it. NOTE: the separate public FlyoutBase.UseNativePopup property is cross-platform (also used by MenuFlyout.UIKit.cs) and is NOT in scope for this Android-only removal.
assessments.notes:
Confirmed the feature is alive and Android-scoped. FeatureConfiguration.Popup.UseNativePopup (FeatureConfiguration.cs:370) is wrapped in #if __ANDROID__ and defaults to false. The whole native path lives behind `_useNativePopup` (Popup.WithPopupRoot.cs:22-23, all branches #if __ANDROID__) routing to PopupWindow-based logic in Popup.Android.cs. Public class NativePopup (NativePopup.Android.cs) extends NativePopupBase (Popup.Legacy.cs) and exposes an Android-only Anchor DP of type Android.Views.View; this is the only genuinely public removable surface and it's Android-only/obscure. NativePopupAdapter<T> is internal. MenuFlyout.Android.cs and ContentDialog.Visuals.cs gate native PopupMenu/PopupWindow behavior on UseNativePopup.

Effort M not S because removal must thread through several partials and the runtime-test helper (FeatureConfigurationHelper.UseNativePopups, Given_Popup.Check_Can_Reach_Main_Visual_Tree_Alternate_Mode, Given_MenuFlyout/Given_ContentDialog native-popup tests) plus the ContentDialog_TextBox sample that toggles the flag — all must be deleted/cleaned, and ContentDialog.Visuals.cs / Popup.WithPopupRoot.cs simplified once the #if __ANDROID__ branches go.

Scope clarification: this is the NATIVE-Android-Views rendering target only. Do NOT remove FlyoutBase.UseNativePopup (FlyoutBase.cs:169) as part of this item — it is not Android-gated, is public, and feeds the UIKit native-menu path (MenuFlyout.UIKit.cs); that is a separate native-iOS concern. If iOS native is also being dropped that public property is a different breaking item.

assessments.summary: Delete the two native-iOS/tvOS-only public types StyleSelector2 and StyleSelectorCollection (in Uno.UI.Views.Controls), which are referenced by nothing else in the repo.
assessments.customerImpact: Practically nobody. These are public but live in the obscure Uno.UI.Views.Controls native-UIKit scaffolding namespace, compile only for native iOS/tvOS (.UIKit.cs), and are not referenced anywhere in the framework, samples, or tests. The only theoretical break is an app that directly instantiated Uno's internal-feeling StyleSelector2/StyleSelectorCollection on iOS — extremely unlikely, and the native iOS render target is being dropped anyway. Note these are distinct from the real public Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.StyleSelector, which is untouched.
assessments.notes: Both files exist and still apply; removal is straightforward. Caveat on naming: the item description says "StyleSelectorCollection.iOS.cs / StyleSelector2.iOS.cs" but the actual files carry the .UIKit.cs suffix (iOS+tvOS), not .iOS.cs — same native Apple intent, just the broader suffix. The two types only reference each other (StyleSelectorCollection : List<StyleSelector2>); a repo-wide grep found zero external usages in src, SamplesApp, or runtime/unit tests. They are part of the legacy native-UIKit bindable-control scaffolding (alongside BindableUIButton, DataTemplateSelectorControl, etc.) and StyleSelector2 derives from DependencyObject (interface on iOS) — pure native rendering-layer code being dropped, so nativeOnly=true and danger=1. No PackageDiffIgnore.xml entry exists for these symbols, so their disappearance will show in the public API diff; if the breaking-change tooling requires it, add ignore entries, but functionally there is no consumer impact. Not the same as the real public StyleSelector API (Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.StyleSelector in StyleSelector.cs + Generated stub), which is unaffected.

assessments.summary: Rename the two misspelled ETW trace-event-ID constants Application.TraceProvider.LauchedStart/LauchedStop to LaunchedStart/LaunchedStop and update their three in-assembly usages.
assessments.customerImpact: Practically no one. These are public const int = 1/2 ETW trace-event IDs nested in Application.TraceProvider, used only to drive WritePhaseEventTrace for native diagnostics. All three references are inside Uno.UI itself (Application.cs declares them; Application.skia.cs and Application.wasm.cs consume them). No tests or external trace tooling in-repo reference the misspelled names. A typical app/library never touches Application.TraceProvider; only a hypothetical custom ETW consumer hard-coding the symbol name would break, and a rename is trivially source-fixable.
assessments.notes: Confirmed via Grep: misspellings "Lauched" still present at Application.cs:157-158 with consumers at Application.skia.cs:153 and Application.wasm.cs:114; only these 3 files match Lauched*/Launched*. TraceProvider is a [Preserve] public static nested class inside the public partial class Application (Application.cs:55, :150), so the consts are public surface but are pure ETW event-ID constants (1/2). Same trace-const pattern recurs across the codebase (FrameworkElement, ImageSource, Storyboard, DependencyObjectStore, ResourceResolver). Not native-only: touches the cross-platform core file plus the Skia and WASM partials, so it does not qualify for the native-drop auto-safe path; danger kept at 2 (not 1) only because it is technically a rename of a public const, but real-world break likelihood is near zero. Fix is mechanical: rename both consts + 2 call sites; builds with no further changes.

assessments.summary: Remove the public binary-compat shim method SetterBase.set_Property(string) from Microsoft.UI.Xaml.SetterBase.
assessments.customerImpact: set_Property is an EditorBrowsable(Never)-hidden binary-compat shim: it exists only so old pre-compiled assemblies that set Setter<T>.Property (which compiles to the IL accessor name set_Property) keep binding after the SetterBase/Setter<T> hierarchy changed. No one writes setterBase.set_Property("x") in source, so source compat is essentially unaffected; the only breakage is a binary-compat break for already-compiled third-party libraries that referenced the old member layout. Realistic exposure for a typical app/library is very low.
assessments.decisionQuestion: set_Property exists solely as a binary-compat shim for the still-shipping public Setter<T> (emitted by XamlFileGenerator and used in tests/docs); its own comment says it can only be removed once Setter<T> is removed. Do we remove set_Property now (accepting the binary-compat break independently) or only as part of a larger Setter<T> removal/rework?
assessments.notes: Confirmed present at src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/SetterBase.cs lines 21-26: `[EditorBrowsable(EditorBrowsableState.Never)] public void set_Property(string name) => OnStringPropertyChanged(name);`. Already hidden via EditorBrowsable. The method delegates to internal OnStringPropertyChanged, overridden only by Setter<T>.OnStringPropertyChanged (Setter.Generic.cs:29) which writes Property=name. The class comment explicitly states "This can be removed in Uno 6 once we remove Setter<T>". Setter<T> is still a live public type: emitted by XamlFileGenerator.cs:6450 for C#-property setters and exercised by unit tests (Given_ItemsControl, Given_ComboBox) and Pivot docs, so the gating prerequisite (dropping Setter<T>) is NOT yet met. Issue #13050 is OPEN, labeled good-first-issue, and a maintainer commented "Still relevant" recently. Not native-rendering code -- core shared Uno.UI Xaml. To fully remove safely, also drop the now-unused OnStringPropertyChanged plumbing and the Setter<T> override. Could also be done as a simple [Obsolete] step before deletion.

assessments.summary: Change Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Duration.TimeSpan from a public mutable field to a public read-only property to match WinUI's surface (and decide what to do about the extra public Type field / public DurationType enum that WinUI does not expose).
assessments.customerImpact: Duration is a niche animation value type. Read access (duration.TimeSpan) stays source-compatible, but field-to-property is a binary-compat break (different IL), and any code doing duration.TimeSpan = x (legal today because it is a mutable field on a struct) would stop compiling. Such mutation is rare and non-idiomatic. WinUI only exposes a read-only TimeSpan property; Uno additionally exposes a public Type field and a public DurationType enum that WinUI keeps internal, so a strict parity pass would also remove those (larger, but separate) break. Very few apps touch Duration.TimeSpan directly and almost none mutate it.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should Duration.TimeSpan become a read-only property to match WinUI (and should the WinUI-divergent public Type field plus public DurationType enum be made internal too), or kept as-is for UWP-parity reasons given the issue notes UWP may differ from WinUI?
assessments.notes: Confirmed in src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Duration.cs lines 18-19: both Type and TimeSpan are public mutable fields. WinUI exposes TimeSpan as a get-only property and keeps DurationType internal (no public Type member). Maintainer (MartinZikmund) confirmed on issue #13096 that 'The discrepancy is still present'; ramezgerges noted UWP may differ from WinUI (referenced PR #13712), which is exactly why this is a confirm/decide item, not a mechanical fix. Internal usages of .TimeSpan are read-only (DependencyProperty.cs:594; AnimatorFactory.cs:17; Timeline.animation.Android.cs:27); the only write (MediaTransportControls.MediaPlayer.cs:149) sits inside an #if !HAS_UNO block, i.e. WinUI-reference-only dead code in Uno, so converting to a read-only property does not break Uno's own build. The Generated stub already says 'Skipping already declared property TimeSpan', so no generated-file conflict. Effort S for the TimeSpan field->property flip; the Type/DurationType removal would be the larger half of the decision.

assessments.summary: Remove the Uno-only `IEnumerable` interface (and its `GetEnumerator()` implementation) from `FrameworkElement` and `ContentControl`, since WinUI does not implement it on these types.
assessments.customerImpact: Compile-break for any app/library that relied on the Uno-specific divergence: `foreach (var child in someElement)`, LINQ over a FrameworkElement/ContentControl, C# collection-initializer syntax (`new StackPanel { childA, childB }`), or assigning `IEnumerable x = element`. This is Uno-only behavior (WinUI never had it), so cross-platform/WinUI-targeting code never depended on it; usage is largely confined to hand-written Uno UI that exploited the convenience. Not used by the XAML source generator (its collection-initializer path keys off IList/ICollection, not bare IEnumerable), so XAML compilation is unaffected. Affects Skia, not just native targets.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in code: `FrameworkElement : IEnumerable` is declared in FrameworkElement.crossruntime.cs:27, .skia.cs:14, .wasm.cs:25, .reference.cs:13 (so present on Skia, NOT native-only); each platform file provides `public IEnumerator GetEnumerator() => _children.GetEnumerator();`. `ContentControl : IEnumerable` is declared unconditionally at ContentControl.cs:35, but its own `Add(View)`/`GetEnumerator()` are gated `#if __ANDROID__` (ContentControl.cs:495-513, returning the single Content as a 1-element sequence); on Skia/WASM the interface is satisfied via the inherited FrameworkElement.GetEnumerator(). Verified WinUI C++ sources (D:\Work\microsoft-ui-xaml2\src) have no such IEnumerable on these types — this is a deliberate Uno divergence for C# collection-initializer/foreach convenience. Removal is mechanical (delete `: IEnumerable` from the partial headers + the GetEnumerator/Add members). Panel.Android.cs also implements IEnumerable for the same reason but is native-only and inherits from FrameworkElement; consider it together for consistency. effort S = small edit, but it is a genuine public-surface removal so it must be in the major version.

assessments.summary: Remove the Android-only public API method `Window.IsStatusBarTranslucent()` (Window.Android.cs); the ComboBox consumer would call the internal NativeWindowWrapper helper instead, which stays.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks any Android app/library that calls the public `Window.IsStatusBarTranslucent()` method directly. This is an Android-only public method (not in WinUI, not on other platforms), niche and undocumented for direct use — practically no typical app depends on it. The internal NativeWindowWrapper.IsStatusBarTranslucent() (used by native Android GetVisualBounds insets and ComboBox dropdown placement) is unaffected and can remain. Since native Android Views rendering is being dropped this major anyway, both the public method and its render-path consumers are slated for removal regardless.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should the public Android-only `Window.IsStatusBarTranslucent()` be removed outright (repointing ComboBox.custom.cs to the internal NativeWindowWrapper helper), or is it moot because the whole native-Android render path — including the only consumers — is being deleted in this major anyway?
assessments.notes: Located: public method only at src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Window/Window.Android.cs:9 (`public bool IsStatusBarTranslucent() => NativeWindowWrapper.Instance.IsStatusBarTranslucent();`) carrying the self-flagging `//TODO: Can remove as breaking change? #8339`. It exists ONLY in the .Android.cs partial — no cross-platform or other-platform declaration — so it is Android-only public surface. Two consumers: (1) internal NativeWindowWrapper.Android.cs:177 GetVisualBounds() uses the internal helper for translucent status-bar inset math (native Android render); (2) ComboBox.custom.cs:799 calls the PUBLIC Window.CurrentSafe!.IsStatusBarTranslucent() inside #if __ANDROID__ to offset the dropdown under a translucent status bar, gated by FeatureConfiguration.ComboBox.AllowPopupUnderTranslucentStatusBar (FeatureConfiguration.cs:101, documented Android-specific in doc/articles/feature-flags.md:61). The method is NOT dead code, but all of it is native-Android-rendering-only. Removing the public method only requires repointing the ComboBox call to the internal NativeWindowWrapper.Instance.IsStatusBarTranslucent(); the internal helper need not be removed. Danger=2 (obscure Android-only public method customers almost never touch); nativeOnly=true (all touched code is native Android Views rendering being dropped). Not auto-danger-1 only because it IS public surface, however niche. Compile/runtime not validated — assessment by code inspection.

assessments.summary: Remove the throwing `new` setters on `Count` and `IsReadOnly` (and ideally the whole binary-compat `new`-shadow block) in `ColorKeyFrameCollection`, letting the get-only base `DependencyObjectCollection<T>` members show through.
assessments.customerImpact: Effectively no one. `Count`/`IsReadOnly` setters here only ever `throw new NotSupportedException()`, so no functioning code calls them. After removal the base `DependencyObjectCollection<T>.Count`/`IsReadOnly` (get-only, identical read behavior) still satisfy `IList<ColorKeyFrame>`/`ICollection`. Binary-breaking only (the shadow members vanish from this type's metadata); source-compatible for any realistic consumer. Aligns the surface with WinUI, where these are plain get-only collection members.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Remove only the two throwing setters (`Count`/`IsReadOnly`), or delete the entire `new`-shadow "binary backward compatibility" block (Size, GetEnumerator, Add, Clear, Contains, CopyTo, Remove, IndexOf, Insert, RemoveAt, this[], Count, IsReadOnly) so `ColorKeyFrameCollection` matches its zero-shadow siblings `DoubleKeyFrameCollection`/`ObjectKeyFrameCollection`?
assessments.notes: Confirmed in repo: lines 27-37 of src/Uno.UI/UI/Xaml/Media/Animation/ColorKeyFrameCollection.cs declare `public new int Count { get; set => throw NotSupportedException }` and `public new bool IsReadOnly { get; set => throw ... }`. The file's own comment (line 12) says these `new` members exist "for binary backward compatibility ... can be safely removed in an eventual breaking new version" — i.e. they were explicitly parked for exactly this breaking release. Base `DependencyObjectCollection<T>` (DependencyObjectCollection.cs:122,124) already provides get-only `Count`/`IsReadOnly` with identical behavior. ColorKeyFrameCollection is the ONLY KeyFrameCollection carrying these shadows — DoubleKeyFrameCollection and ObjectKeyFrameCollection derive from the same base with no `new` members, so the cleanest fix is deleting the whole block (hence needsDecision). Only internal use (ColorAnimationUsingKeyFrames.cs) constructs/references the type and never calls the shadowed setters; the two Uno.WinAppSDKSyncGenerator hits are API-surface tooling. Not native rendering and not Uno.UWP/Foundation, so judged on normal merit; danger 1 because the setters are pure throw-only dead surface. Validation: code review only (not compiled).

assessments.summary: A fix to the internal WinUI/UWP API sync-generator build tool (which regenerates the [NotImplemented] Generated/ stubs); the tool PR #13867 patched (UWPSyncGenerator) no longer exists and has been superseded by Uno.WinAppSDKSyncGenerator.
assessments.customerImpact: No customer impact. This is a maintainer-only, build-time Exe (Uno.WinAppSDKSyncGenerator) that regenerates the Generated/ NotImplemented stub folders.
