It ships in no NuGet package and exposes no public API. The specific code PR #13867 touched (the old UWPSyncGenerator) is not present in the repo at all.
assessments.notes: PR #13867 ("fix: WinUI sync generator", CLOSED, by Youssef1313, labeled stale) modified the old UWPSyncGenerator/DocGenerator tooling to emit a WinUI/WinAppSDK API surface. Searched the repo: there is NO UWPSyncGenerator project or any reference to that name (grep for "UWPSyncGenerator" = 0 hits). The sync generator has since been fully renamed/replaced by src/Uno.WinAppSDKSyncGenerator (Exe, TargetFramework=NetCurrent, OutputType=Exe), which already generates the WinAppSDK surface — i.e. the intent of #13867 is already realized through different code, and the specific PR diff no longer applies. This is not a breaking change for consumers under any reading: it is internal maintainer tooling with no public surface and no runtime/rendering impact (so not nativeOnly either). I'd treat this item as a no-op / obsolete with respect to the breaking-changes effort; nothing to ship or remove for customers. Marked currentState=obsolete because the referenced change is superseded; danger 1 per rubric (dead/legacy build-only code, no public surface).

assessments.summary: Remove the public static FeatureConfiguration.VisualState.ApplySettersBeforeTransition backward-compat flag and its two branches in VisualStateGroup, keeping only the WinUI-correct "apply setters after transition" path.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks only apps that explicitly set FeatureConfiguration.VisualState.ApplySettersBeforeTransition = true (a public static property). The flag defaults to false, produces non-Windows behavior when true, and its own XML doc says it "is for backward compatibility with old versions of uno and should not be turned on." Realistic dependency is near-zero; any app referencing it gets a compile error and its VSM setter-timing reverts to the correct WinUI behavior (setters applied after the transition storyboard).
assessments.notes: Confirmed live in repo: property at FeatureConfiguration.cs:792 (public static, inside `public static class VisualState`); consumed at VisualStateGroup.cs:308 (apply-before branch) and :362 (the !flag guard inside ApplyTargetState's local ApplyTargetState/ApplyTargetStateSetters). Only 3 files reference it (the two above plus one test). Removal = delete the property, delete the line-308 `if` block, drop the `!ApplySettersBeforeTransition` guard at :362 so setters always apply after the transition, and remove the test `When_TransitionAndSetter_InCompatibilityMode_Then_SetterAppliedBefore` (which is the only consumer that sets it true, with a finally reset to false). Issue #13326 is OPEN, labeled kind/breaking-change + difficulty/starter; thread (Youssef1313/jeromelaban) confirms the flag is used but not expected to be wanted by any app and that the true-path doesn't match Windows; maintainers said "keep it for now" historically, and Martin marked the issue "Relevant", so it is a valid candidate for this major-version breaking pass. Cross-platform core code, not native rendering, so nativeOnly=false. No dependency on DependencyObject-as-class or any other BC item.

assessments.summary: Remove the no-op AdjustArrange(Size) member (and its dead AdjustArrangePartial hook) from IFrameworkElement, the per-target FrameworkElement.Interface.* stubs, the native mixin generators, and its sole call site in the legacy Layouter.
assessments.customerImpact: Practically nobody. AdjustArrange is a pure no-op (always returns its input). It is declared on the INTERNAL IFrameworkElement interface, so it is not public API there. The only way it leaks publicly is the native-UIKit mixin generator, which emits `public virtual Size AdjustArrange(Size)` on FrameworkElement for native iOS/tvOS — a target being dropped this major version. The companion extensibility hook AdjustArrangePartial has zero implementations anywhere in the repo, so no control or customer code overrides behavior through it. A typical app/library never calls or overrides this.
assessments.notes: Issue #14478 is OPEN; the code still exists exactly as described ("exists and always returns the same size"). Verified locations: declaration at IFrameworkElement.cs:113 (`_Size AdjustArrange(_Size finalSize)`); no-op implementations in FrameworkElement.Interface.skia.cs:60, .reference.cs:62, .wasm.cs:90 (all `return finalSize`); the native mixin generators emit it (Android as explicit interface impl `Size IFrameworkElement.AdjustArrange`, UIKit as `public virtual Size AdjustArrange`). Sole runtime caller is Layouter.cs:739 inside `#if !UNO_REFERENCE_API` (legacy native measure/arrange path). AdjustArrangePartial — the only real extensibility point — has NO implementation anywhere (grep outside generators returns nothing), confirming it is fully dead. One test stub also implements it: NativeViewIFrameworkElement.cs:114. Cleanest sequencing: do this after/with the native-target rendering drop, since the native mixin emitters and Layouter native path are the bulk of the surface; on Skia it is just deleting a dead no-op + its interface slot + the (effectively unreachable) call site. Low risk; the only theoretical break is native-iOS subclasses calling/overriding the public-virtual AdjustArrange, which is a dropped target. Danger 2 (not 1) only because of that technically-public iOS surface.

assessments.summary: Remove the remaining Fluent V1 scaffolding: the public XamlControlsResourcesV1 type, the ControlsResourcesVersion.Version1 enum value, the 4 Uno.UI.FluentTheme.v1 project variants (and their .slnx/Directory.Build.props/slnf/nuspec entries), the source-generator special-casing, and the version-1 branch in GetWinUIThemeResourceUrl.
assessments.customerImpact: Removing the public sealed type Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.XamlControlsResourcesV1 and the public enum value ControlsResourcesVersion.Version1 is a source/binary breaking change for any app that instantiated V1 resources or set ControlsResourcesVersion=Version1. In practice almost nobody depends on this: V1 is already non-functional at runtime (XamlControlsResources.cs line 45 hard-forces Version2, and the V1 style content/Resources directory is already deleted), so apps using V1 are silently getting V2 today. Realistic breakage is limited to the rare app that references the V1 type/enum name in code or XAML.
assessments.decisionQuestion: The V1 style content is already deleted and V1 is forced to V2 at runtime, so the only thing left to "drop" is the public surface (XamlControlsResourcesV1 type + ControlsResourcesVersion.Version1 enum value) plus build/package scaffolding. Do we hard-remove that public surface now (clean break, requires PackageDiffIgnore entries), or keep the stubs as [Obsolete] no-ops for one more cycle?
assessments.notes: The substantive removal is ALREADY DONE: there is no src/Uno.UI.FluentTheme.v1/Resources/Version1 directory (the actual V1 style XAML — the "maintenance nightmare" cited in issue #14765 — is gone), and themeresources_v1.xaml is a 251-byte empty placeholder ResourceDictionary. XamlControlsResources.cs line 45 already forces requestedVersion = ControlsResourcesVersion.Version2 with comment "Force version 2, as 1 is no longer supported". So this item is partially-complete; what remains is removing dead scaffolding/public API. Remaining wiring to clean up: (1) the 4 Uno.UI.FluentTheme.v1 csproj variants, still listed in src/Uno.UI.slnx (lines ~320-329), Directory.Build.props _AdjustedOutputProjects (lines 156-159), and slnf package filters; (2) NuGet packaging — Uno.UI.FluentTheme.v1.dll referenced/shipped across many TFMs in build/nuget/Uno.WinUI.nuspec; (3) source generator special-casing in XamlCodeGeneration.cs (lines 121, 850-851) that skips FluentTheme.v* assemblies; (4) the version==1 branch in XamlFilePathHelper.GetWinUIThemeResourceUrl; (5) public enum value ControlsResourcesVersion.Version1; (6) the [NotImplemented] XamlControlsResourcesV1 type itself. No external (non-FluentTheme.v1) C#/XAML consumers of XamlControlsResourcesV1 or ControlsResourcesVersion.Version1 were found in the repo. Removing public members will need PackageDiffIgnore.xml entries (it already has many FluentTheme.v1 entries). Not native-only: this is cross-platform theming/packaging infrastructure (Skia + WASM + Reference + mobile), not native-target rendering code.

assessments.summary: Delete the redundant public class Uno.Extensions.Vector2Extensions (in Uno.UWP), leaving the functionally identical System.Numerics.VectorExtensions (in Uno.Foundation) as the single Vector2<->Point/Size conversion helper.
assessments.customerImpact: Uno.Extensions.Vector2Extensions is a public static class exposing ToPoint/ToVector2/ToSize. Apps/libraries that call these via extension-method syntax (v.ToPoint(), p.ToVector2()) keep compiling transparently as long as System.Numerics is in scope, because the surviving System.Numerics.VectorExtensions has identical signatures. Only code that references the type by name (Uno.Extensions.Vector2Extensions.ToPoint(v)) breaks. These are niche numeric-conversion helpers a typical app almost never references explicitly, so real-world breakage is very low. Note the surviving VectorExtensions carries a 'do not remove, third parties may depend on it' comment; Vector2Extensions does not.
assessments.notes: Confirmed both files exist and are functionally identical. Vector2Extensions (Uno.UWP) is `public static class` in namespace `Uno.Extensions`; VectorExtensions (Uno.Foundation) is `public static partial class` in namespace `System.Numerics` and is the WinAppSDK-ref counterpart (header comment: 'Please do not remove it as third-parties may depend on it.'). All 21 in-repo callers of .ToVector2()/.ToPoint()/.ToSize() import `using System.Numerics;` (e.g. PointerRoutedEventArgs.cs, BorderVisual.skia.cs), i.e. they already consume VectorExtensions, NOT Vector2Extensions; the in-repo build does not depend on the file being removed. History: 2023 commit c583033d19 'Remove duplicated extensions methods' deleted the old internal Uno.UI/Extensions/VectorExtensions.cs and expanded Vector2Extensions; later 3063947d87/bb99c8d24c re-added System.Numerics.VectorExtensions. So Vector2Extensions is now the leftover duplicate. STALE METADATA: build/PackageDiffIgnore.xml lines 1633-1634 reference an outdated signature (ToVector / params v,p) that no longer matches the current file (ToVector2 / params vector,point); those two ignore entries should be removed when the file is deleted. Not native-only (touches Uno.UWP public API, which is being kept). Consider adding a [Uno.Extensions] obsolete-forwarding shim if preserving binary/source compat for the named-type case is desired, but given identical extension methods survive in System.Numerics, a straight deletion is reasonable.

assessments.summary: Change TimePickerFlyoutPresenter's base class from FlyoutPresenter (ContentControl) to Control, matching the WinUI IDL and the sibling DatePickerFlyoutPresenter, and drop the now-redundant `new` on IsDefaultShadowEnabled/IsDefaultShadowEnabledProperty.
assessments.customerImpact: Breaks anyone who casts/treats a TimePickerFlyoutPresenter as a FlyoutPresenter or ContentControl (e.g. accessing .Content, or an implicit `Style TargetType="FlyoutPresenter"` that currently also flows onto it), and removes the inherited FlyoutPresenter/ContentControl members from the public surface. This is niche: the presenter is template-driven and almost always only styled/templated (Control-level props), so a typical app is unaffected. WinUI itself derives it from Control, so apps written against real WinUI never depended on the ContentControl hierarchy.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in-repo: src/Uno.UI/.../TimePickerFlyoutPresenter.cs line 8 declares `public partial class TimePickerFlyoutPresenter : FlyoutPresenter`, and FlyoutPresenter : ContentControl : Control. WinUI reference (D:/Work/microsoft-ui-xaml2/dxaml/phone/idl/Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Phone.idl lines 346-351) declares `runtimeclass TimePickerFlyoutPresenter : Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Controls.Control` — so the change DOES still apply and brings Uno into WinUI parity. The sibling DatePickerFlyoutPresenter (DatePickerFlyoutPresenter_Partial.cs line 15) ALREADY correctly derives from Control, confirming this is just an unfixed inconsistency. Mechanics: (1) change base to Control; (2) drop the `new` keyword on IsDefaultShadowEnabled and IsDefaultShadowEnabledProperty in TimePickerFlyoutPresenter.Properties.cs (they currently shadow FlyoutPresenter's own IsDefaultShadowEnabled — once base is Control there is nothing to shadow); (3) the InitializeImpl comment in partial.mux.cs already references RuntimeClass Control, consistent with the target. Generic.xaml style (lines 1842+) only sets Control-level properties (Width/Font/Background/BorderBrush/BorderThickness/IsTabStop/Template), so the style/template stays valid. Generated stub TimePickerFlyoutPresenter.cs is already in #if false form, no edit needed. Validation here: code review only (no build run). Effort S — a 3-line change plus regenerating goldens/sync-generator surface if it tracks the base type.

assessments.summary: Change Android drawable retargeting + DrawableHelper to feed the file extension through the resource-name encoder (GetFileNameWithoutExtension -> GetFileName), so same-name images with different extensions (logo.png vs logo.jpg) get distinct drawable keys instead of colliding.
assessments.customerImpact: Android apps that bundle two assets with the same base name but different extensions (e.g. Image.png + Image.jpg) currently lose one (issue #9618, still OPEN); this fixes that. Breaking aspect: the generated drawable resource key format changes (the extension is now routed through the dot-replacing Encode, e.g. an effective key change from a literal-dot name to logo_png), altering the generated R.drawable.* map entries. Apps that resolve assets by the normal ms-appx:/// URI are unaffected (encoder change is symmetric build-time + runtime). Only customers who reference generated drawable names directly or depend on the old name-without-extension behavior would break. Skia-on-Android also goes through DrawableHelper/StorageFile.Android.cs for ms-appx image assets, so this is NOT dropped-native-rendering code.
assessments.notes: Verified the original PR-15891 commit (ae0ff582d11, title "fix: Include original file extension in retargeted drawables on Android") exists in the object DB but is NOT an ancestor of HEAD (sits on an abandoned branch referencing PR #1353, not the uno repo). HEAD's AndroidResourceNameEncoder.cs still uses the old GetFileNameWithoutExtension path -> change NOT yet applied. Linked bug #9618 is still OPEN. The diff is two-sided and self-consistent: build-time AndroidResourceNameEncoder.EncodePath gains an isDrawable flag (uses GetFileName so extension goes through Encode -> dot becomes underscore), and runtime DrawableHelper.FindResourceId switches GetFileNameWithoutExtension -> GetFileName so lookup keys match. Existing unit tests in Given_AndroidResourceConverter.cs (When_Convert + When_EncodeDrawablePath DataRows currently expecting literal-dot names like Assets_logo.png / logo.png) must be regenerated to the dot-replaced form; that test churn plus adding a same-name/diff-extension regression case is the bulk of the effort, hence S. Danger 3 (not 1): although Android-only, it is in the asset packaging/resolution layer that Skia-on-Android still consumes (StorageFile.Android.cs ms-appx resolution + DrawableHelper), and it changes the format of generated R.drawable.* keys, which a minority of advanced apps could reference. The 9-patch (.9.png) special-casing in Encode interacts with feeding the extension through Encode and should be re-checked when implementing.

assessments.summary: Move the public Microsoft.Windows.ApplicationModel.Resources (MRT Core) types out of the Uno.UI assembly into Uno.UWP (or a dedicated MRT-Core assembly), so the API ships from its correct, isolated WinAppSDK component location.
assessments.customerImpact: Public types under Microsoft.Windows.ApplicationModel.Resources (ResourceLoader, ResourceManager, ResourceContext, ResourceMap, ResourceCandidate, etc.) keep identical names/namespaces, so source and PackageReference consumers recompile transparently. Breakage is limited to binary/reflection consumers that bind to the type by its current assembly (Uno.UI) — e.g. Assembly.Load / typeof().Assembly checks or assembly-qualified type names. This MRT-Core API is a niche WinAppSDK surface; most Uno apps use Windows.ApplicationModel.Resources.ResourceLoader, x:Uid, or .resw indirection instead, and the only Uno.UI type with a real (non-NotImplemented) body is the ResourceLoader wrapper. So a typical app/library is unlikely to depend on its assembly location.
assessments.decisionQuestion: Should these MRT-Core types land in Uno.UWP (matches the existing Windows.ApplicationModel.Resources.ResourceLoader they wrap, and is where Generator.cs says the "correct location would be") or in a brand-new dedicated assembly to mirror WinAppSDK's isolated MRT-Core component? Uno.UWP is the lower-effort, lower-risk choice; a separate assembly is a larger packaging change.
assessments.notes: Confirmed in-repo: the hand-written types live in src/Uno.UI/Windows/ApplicationModel/Resources/ and the generated [NotImplemented] stubs in src/Uno.UI/Generated/3.0.0.0/Microsoft.Windows.ApplicationModel.Resources/. The sync generator hardcodes this placement and EXPLICITLY documents the problem at Generator.cs lines 429-434: "Microsoft.Windows.ApplicationModel.Resources: Correct location would be Uno.UWP, but was previously generated in Uno.UI." So the work still applies (not done). Feasibility: Uno.UWP does NOT reference Uno.UI (the reference is one-way: Uno.UI -> Uno.UWP, plus Uno.UWP Compile-Includes a few specific Uno.UI files), and the wrapped global::Windows.ApplicationModel.Resources.ResourceLoader already lives in Uno.UWP — so the move into Uno.UWP introduces no circular reference. No Uno.UI-internal consumers reference these types (grep found none), so moving them out won't break Uno.UI internally. The move also requires updating GetFolderForNamespace and the namespace allow-lists in Uno.WinAppSDKSyncGenerator/Generator.cs so regeneration emits into the new assembly. Danger 3 (not 4): public surface but a niche MRT-Core API; only assembly-identity/binary consumers break, source/PackageReference consumers are unaffected.

assessments.summary: Remove or make-internal the ~173 public legacy Windows.UI.Input.* types (PointerPoint, GestureRecognizer, ManipulationDelta, RadialController, Spatial.*, Inking.*, Preview.Injection.*) so only the modern Microsoft.UI.Input.* surface remains public.
assessments.customerImpact: Apps/libraries ported from UWP that still write `Windows.UI.Input.PointerPoint`/`GestureRecognizer`/`ManipulationDelta` etc. fail to compile. That is a very common UWP idiom, so the blast radius on legacy-ported codebases is real and broad. Mitigating factor: in the modern WinUI flavor these Windows.UI.Input.* types are duplicate [Uno.NotImplemented] stubs shadowed by the working Microsoft.UI.Input.* equivalents, so any app actually *calling* them already throws NotImplementedException at runtime today — removing them converts a runtime-throw into a compile error and pushes users to the correct Microsoft.UI.Input namespace. Net: high compile-break breadth, low loss of real functionality.
assessments.decisionQuestion: For the ~173 Windows.UI.Input.* public types: hard-remove them, or only flip them to internal (less breaking)? And specifically what to do with the REAL (non-stub) Windows.UI.Input.Preview.Injection.InputInjector family that test infra consumes unconditionally — move it to Microsoft.UI.Input.Preview.Injection and update call sites, or keep it as the lone surviving Windows.UI.Input.* surface?
assessments.notes: Repo is now WinUI-flavor only (HAS_UNO_WINUI always defined in src/Uno.CrossTargetting.targets). The real input types live in src/Uno.UI/UI/Input/WinRT/ and are emitted as Microsoft.UI.Input.* via `#if IS_UNO_UI_PROJECT` (else Windows.UI.Input). The duplicate public surface comes from src/Uno.UWP/Generated/3.0.0.0/Windows.UI.Input*/ (~173 public [Uno.NotImplemented] stub types: PointerPoint, PointerPointProperties, GestureRecognizer, Manipulation*, RadialController*, EdgeGesture, plus Spatial.* and Inking.* subnamespaces) — these are compiled unconditionally and are exactly the noise #18875 wants gone. STILL APPLIES (verified stubs present and public). nativeOnly=false: this is Uno.UWP WinRT public-API surface, NOT native-target rendering code. Big caveat found: Windows.UI.Input.Preview.Injection.InputInjector / IInputInjectorTarget / InjectedInput* are NOT stubs — they are real implementations (15 files under src/Uno.UWP/UI/Input/Preview.Injection/) consumed unconditionally by test infra (Given_InputManager.cs line 14 `using Windows.UI.Input.Preview.Injection;`, all GestureRecognizerTests samples). So this is not a clean namespace delete; the injection family needs a deliberate plan (move + update call sites) separate from the pure-stub removal. Issue is labeled difficulty/starter, but the InputInjector entanglement makes it more than mechanical. Independent of DependencyObject-as-class (BC26).

assessments.summary: Removes obsolete #if __ANDROID_NN__ (API < 31) preprocessor branches and normalizes __ANDROID_23__ guards to __ANDROID__ across ~23 Android files, since Uno now compiles against Android 12/13+ only.
assessments.customerImpact: Effectively none. These were compile-time branches selecting code for Android API levels below the minimum supported (31). The dead arms never compiled in supported builds. The only public-surface-adjacent change is the `new Brush Foreground` modifier on Control/ContentPresenter/TextBlock/IconElement/TextBoxView, which is kept identical (just guarded by __ANDROID__ instead of __ANDROID_23__) — so the emitted public API is unchanged. A removed dead `BindableGridView.ColumnWidth`/`SetColumnWidth` override only existed under !__ANDROID_16__ (never compiled). No typical app/library depends on any of this.
assessments.notes: Verified against the live worktree: grep for `__ANDROID_\d+__` across all of src returns ZERO matches, so every numbered conditional this PR targeted is already gone. Spot-checked the representative cases: BindableGridView.Android.cs no longer has the `SetColumnWidth`/`ColumnWidth` block; ActivityHelper.Android.cs has `ConfigChanges.LayoutDirection` unconditional; all 5 cross-platform `Foreground` declarations (Control, ContentPresenter, TextBlock, IconElement, TextBoxView.Android) now use `#if __ANDROID__ new #endif` rather than `__ANDROID_23__`. Two of the touched files live in Uno.UWP (PhoneCallManager/PackageId/Launcher = platform WinRT APIs, NOT being dropped) but the edits there were purely dead-arm removal with no behavioral or API change. nativeOnly=false because the change spans cross-platform Uno.UI files and Uno.UWP platform-API files, not exclusively native-rendering code — but it is still danger 1 as it is pure dead-conditional cleanup. Nothing to ship: the desired end-state is fully present on this branch.

assessments.summary: Refactors the internal Border/ContentPresenter/Panel/Page border-rendering pipeline to share a BorderLayerRenderer driven by an IBorderInfoProvider + BorderLayerState, plus adds FauxGradientBorderPresenter for gradient-border simulation.
assessments.customerImpact: No public API changes. BorderLayerRenderer, IBorderInfoProvider, and BorderLayerState are all internal; consumers (Border, ContentPresenter, Panel, Page) keep their public surface unchanged. Customers cannot reference these types, so nothing breaks. The bulk of the churn was native-target rendering plumbing (Border.Android/iOS/macOS/wasm, ContentPresenter/Panel/Page native partials), which is maintenance-only / being dropped in the Skia-only direction. A theme-resource cleanup (FauxGradientBorderPresenter replacing per-platform gradient-border hacks) is visual-only.
assessments.notes: PR #12593 is CLOSED, but the refactor it proposed has already landed on this branch. Commit 3887af4454 ("refactor: Move border-related properties into a IBorderInfoProvider") plus follow-ups (a0018d8a1d, 3c16c1fc64, f32e017186, dced5e6424) implemented it. Confirmed in-tree: IBorderInfoProvider.cs and BorderLayerState.cs exist; BorderLayerRenderer.cs uses _borderInfoProvider + BorderLayerState; FauxGradientBorderPresenter.cs and LinearGradientBrush.faux.cs exist; the PR-deleted mocks (BorderLayerRenderer.reference.cs, BorderLayerRenderer.unittests.cs, Mock/Border.reference.cs) are gone. The only structural drift from the PR is file naming evolution (PR's BorderLayerRenderer.iOSmacOS.cs is now BorderLayerRenderer.Apple.cs), which is post-merge maintenance, not pending work. Nothing remains to ship for this item.

assessments.summary: A closed WIP perf PR proposed replacing the closure-based Brush.AssignAndObserveBrush subscription with a delegate/closure-free SubscribeToChanges/BrushChangedCallback mechanism; the codebase has since been rewritten so both the old and proposed APIs no longer exist.
assessments.customerImpact: Effectively none. The PR touched internal members only: Brush.AssignAndObserveBrush was internal-ish (public static historically but consumed only by Uno's own TextBlock/TextBox), and the proposed SubscribeToChanges/BrushChangedCallback were all internal. Both the old and the proposed surface are gone from the current tree, replaced by internal SetupBrushChanged + RegisterInvalidateRender weak-event plumbing. No typical app or library subscribes to brush changes through these APIs.
assessments.notes: PR #12595 is CLOSED (WIP perf, authored by MartinZikmund, replaced #12234). It added an internal closure-free brush-change subscription (SubscribeToChanges, BrushChangedCallback delegate, BrushChangedDisposable, RaiseBrushChanged) and migrated TextBlock/TextBox off Brush.AssignAndObserveBrush. Verified in this worktree: neither AssignAndObserveBrush, SupportsAssignAndObserveBrush, SubscribeToChanges, BrushChangedCallback, nor BrushChangedDisposable exist anywhere in source (git grep). Brush.cs now lives in the Microsoft.UI.Xaml.Media namespace (was Windows.UI.Xaml.Media) and uses an entirely different design: RegisterInvalidateRender via WeakEventHelper.WeakEventCollection plus internal static SetupBrushChanged, consumed by TextBlock.cs (Brush.SetupBrushChanged at line 549). The only residual hit for "AssignAndObserveBrush" is a stale token string in src/SamplesApp/SamplesApp.Skia.WebAssembly.Browser/aot.profile, not real code. The perf intent (avoid per-subscription closures/delegates) is already realized by the current weak-event mechanism. No action needed for the breaking-changes effort; this item is superseded by later refactors. Not native-only: Brush.cs is core cross-platform Uno.UI code.

phases.name: Phase 1 - Native-only removals & inert dead-code (danger 1)
phases.rationale: Safest vanguard: removals scoped to native Android/iOS/UIKit/WASM-DOM targets being dropped, plus orphaned/dead public stubs with zero consumers. These cannot affect Skia consumers' running code. All danger 1, mostly effort S; ship as one big batch. The one decision-flagged item (BC67) is a throwing-setter cleanup, otherwise mechanical.

phases.name: Phase 2 - Native-only API surface removals (danger 2, nativeOnly)
phases.rationale: Native-target-scoped public API removals that need a small consumer redirect or a deprecation decision, but still can't touch Skia. Slightly riskier than Phase 1 because they delete public members callers might reference (BC23 native-view hosting, BC66 Android status-bar API). Both need a deprecate-vs-remove decision.

phases.name: Phase 3 - Internal feature-flag, dead-code & shim removals (danger 2)
phases.rationale: Cross-target but low-blast-radius: deleting always-on/always-off feature flags by inlining the live branch, removing dead/orphaned helpers, binary-compat shims, and no-op members. Behavior is unchanged for anyone on defaults; the break is only for code touching the flag/shim itself. Batch the pure flag-inlines (BC06, BC46, BC69, BC33) and the dead-code deletes together. BC44/BC63/BC37 touch the Setter pipeline and should land adjacently.

phases.name: Phase 4 - Visibility reductions & signature/rename tweaks (danger 2)
phases.rationale: Tighten Uno-only public surface to match WinUI (make internal/protected/sealed, explicit-interface, hide from IntelliSense) and small signature fixes. Each is narrow and independent; the break only hits code that referenced the over-exposed member. Effort S throughout. Group the constructor/setter visibility reductions (BC08, BC11, BC12) and the typo/rename fixes (BC62, BC41, BC28, BC64) for efficient review.

phases.name: Phase 5 - Behavioral defaults, base-class & type tweaks (danger 3)
phases.rationale: Wider-reaching but still localized: visibility reductions on more-derivable hooks (BC17, BC19, BC34, BC36), per-type base-class realignments (BC27, BC73, BC52), enum/type-shape changes (BC13, BC65), and behavior-changing defaults (BC50 culture, BC74 drawable keys, BC42 xmlns, BC51 URI rewrite, BC21 unclear member, BC75 MRT move). These can silently change runtime behavior or break parity, so they follow all the surface-only changes. Several need a decision before proceeding.

phases.name: Phase 6 - Larger structural moves & legacy-mechanism removal (danger 3-4)
phases.rationale: Heavier-effort structural changes that ripple across many files: removing the legacy templated-parent mechanism (BC01), the precedence-enum repurposing (BC39), Fluent V1 scaffolding teardown (BC71), the Uno.UI.Toolkit assembly/namespace rename (BC53), the legacy Windows.UI.Input.* surface removal (BC76), and the Background DP relocation onto Control (BC38). These gate or interact with the Phase 7 type-system overhaul and must land before it. BC38 (FrameworkElement.Background -> Control) is a precursor to the FrameworkElement/Control hierarchy work.

phases.name: Phase 7 - Pervasive type-system & DataContext/DependencyObject changes (danger 4-5)
phases.rationale: Most dangerous, ship last: the DataContext-DP narrowing (BC54 symptom, BC58 generator root-cause), the DependencyObject interface-to-base-class conversion (BC26), and the UserControl base-class realignment (BC14). These rewrite the generator-emitted DO mixin and the type hierarchy every control inherits, with the broadest possible blast radius. Sequence BC58 (stop emitting DataContext everywhere) before BC54 (its FlyoutBase symptom), and treat BC26 + BC14 as the final, separately-stabilized landings.

crossItemDependencies.why: BC54 (FlyoutBase.DataContext non-public) is explicitly a symptom of Uno emitting a public DataContext DP on every DependencyObject. BC58 removes the DataContext emission from DependencyObjectGenerator and restricts it to FrameworkElement, which is the real fix that makes BC54 fall out. Doing BC54 alone would be a band-aid; do the generator change first.

BC26 converts DependencyObject from a generator-mixin interface into a real base class. BC58 changes what that same generator emits (DataContext). Settling the generator's member set (BC58) before re-rooting the type hierarchy (BC26) avoids re-doing the DataContext placement work against a moving base class.

BC14 realigns the Control->UserControl->Page hierarchy; BC38 moves Background off FrameworkElement onto Control and re-declares it on background-painting subclasses. Landing the Background relocation first means UserControl's new direct-Control inheritance already sees Background in the right place, avoiding a double migration of the same property.

Both touch the Style Setter pipeline: BC37 removes the source-gen fallback that emits a Setter<T> for CLR (non-DP) properties, and BC44 removes the Setter<T> type itself plus its SetterBase shims. Remove the generator's remaining producer of Setter<T> (BC37) before/with deleting Setter<T> (BC44) so generation never targets a deleted type.

BC63 removes SetterBase.set_Property(string), one of the back-compat shims BC44 lists as having been forced onto SetterBase by Setter<T>. Keep them adjacent (BC44 then BC63) so the shim's only reason-to-exist is gone before it is deleted.

decisionQuestions.question: Are all internal/external consumers fully migrated to the NewFrameworkTemplateBuilder/TemplateMaterializationSettings path so the public FrameworkTemplateBuilder delegate and the legacy (owner, FrameworkTemplateBuilder) constructor overloads on FrameworkTemplate/ControlTemplate/DataTemplate/ItemsPanelTemplate can be deleted without breaking template instantiation?

Confirm native routed-event bubbling is fully gone on all shipped targets so the public EventsBubblingInManagedCode DP (and its property/coerce callback) is truly meaningless and safe to remove rather than just hide.

Are the bootstrapper meta-packages (Uno.WinUI.Skia.X11/MacOS/Linux.FrameBuffer and Uno.WinUI.WebAssembly) already removed (currentState possibly-done)? If not, can templates and downstream PackageReferences be repointed to the Runtime.* packages without breaking existing consumer projects?
Should ComboBox.OnIsDropDownOpenChanged(bool,bool) become private protected or fully private, and are there any internal/derived overrides relying on the current protected virtual signature?

Confirmed obsolete: investigation shows ItemsControl.OnItemsChanged already matches WinUI exactly, so the proposed object->IVectorChangedEventArgs / visibility change would break parity. Drop entirely, or is there a residual concern to capture?

Reparenting UserControl from ContentControl to Control changes its inherited members (e.g. ContentTemplate, content alignment) and Content type to UIElement; how do we handle existing user code/XAML that relies on UserControl's current ContentControl surface?

Re-applying the reverted PropertyChangedParams struct to OnPropertyChanged2 / IDependencyObjectInternal / DependencyObjectGenerator was reverted once before — what caused the revert, and is the regression now addressed before re-enabling PropertyChangedParams.cs?

Removing FeatureConfiguration.FrameworkElement.UseLegacyHitTest permanently locks WinUI-aligned hit-testing; are there known scenarios still relying on the legacy opt-in path that need a migration note?
Should FrameworkPropertyMetadata.DefaultUpdateSourceTrigger merely become internal, or should the whole FrameworkPropertyMetadata class be made internal? Confirm no public API exposes it.

The member at AutomationProperties.cs ~line 44 is AnnotationsProperty, a legitimate public WinUI attached property, not legacy code. Should this item be dropped, or was a different member intended for removal?

For VisualTreeHelper.AdaptNative/TryAdaptNative on native Android/iOS: deprecate-then-remove with an [Obsolete] cycle, or remove outright in this major since the native rendering targets are being dropped?

Should Border.ChildProperty DependencyProperty be made internal or removed entirely? Confirm no XAML/binding scenarios bind to Child via the DP that would break if it disappears from the public surface.

Converting DependencyObject from a public interface to a real base class on cross-runtime targets is highly pervasive — what is the migration story for any external code that implements the DependencyObject interface today, and how do native Android/iOS (where DO is an interface by necessity) stay compatible?

Hard-coding the dirty-path layout optimization (removing UseInvalidateMeasurePath/UseInvalidateArrangePath) deletes the full-propagation fallback — is there any remaining scenario where the fallback was still needed for correctness?
Should the base ContentPresenter.OnVerticalContentAlignmentChanged be narrowed from protected virtual to private protected, and do any derived types outside the framework override it?
Reduce ContentPresenter.ContentTemplateRoot to internal — confirm no sample, test, or consumer code reads ContentTemplateRoot publicly before tightening it.

Moving Background from FrameworkElement to Control requires re-declaring it on every non-Control subclass that paints a background (Panel, Border, ContentPresenter, TextBlock, RichTextBlock, Shape, Glyphs, CalendarViewBaseItem). Is that subclass list exhaustive, and how do we ensure binding/animation targeting FrameworkElement.Background keeps working?

Renaming DependencyPropertyValuePrecedences.ExplicitStyle to ExplicitOrImplicitStyle and repurposing ImplicitStyle onto DefaultStyle changes precedence semantics — confirm the [EditorBrowsable(Never)] aliases preserve binary/source compat and that no internal call site depends on the old numeric ordering.

Removing lenient clr-namespace: xmlns acceptance will break XAML ported from WPF/Silverlight that still uses it. Is a hard break acceptable in this major, or do we need a one-version deprecation warning first?

Deleting the op_Explicit binary-compat shim on Microsoft.UI.Input.PointerPoint breaks old binaries that bound to the explicit cast. Is a source-recompile acceptable for the major, or must binary compat be preserved?

Hard-coding Center/Center content alignment (removing UseLegacyContentAlignment) changes layout for any app that relied on the legacy Left/Top fallback — do we need a release-notes migration callout or visual-diff validation?
Confirm Uno.Collections.MaterializableList<T> is not referenced by any public API signature before making it internal.

Flipping UseLegacyPrimaryLanguageOverride to false means setting PrimaryLanguageOverride no longer immediately changes CurrentCulture (restart-to-apply, matching WinUI). Is this behavioral change acceptable, and do apps relying on immediate culture switching need a documented workaround?

Filling RewriteUri's empty else-branch to rewrite relative URIs to ms-resource:/// changes how relative System.Uri/ImageSource literals resolve on non-framework owners — confirm WinUI-aligned behavior and that existing samples/apps relying on verbatim emission won't break.

Reparenting RadioMenuFlyoutItem from ToggleMenuFlyoutItem to MenuFlyoutItem requires porting the toggle behavior it currently inherits. Is the ported toggle logic validated for parity, and confirm the _skipBaseTypes SyncGenerator edit emits the correct base?
What is the new name for the renamed Uno.UI.Toolkit assembly/root namespace/XAML xmlns, and how do we handle existing user XAML using:Uno.UI.Toolkit and code references during the major upgrade?

Since FlyoutBase.DataContext being public is a symptom of the generator-wide DataContext emission (BC58), confirm BC54 is fully subsumed by BC58 or specify what FlyoutBase-specific work remains after BC58 lands.

Replace deprecated PreferenceManager.GetDefaultSharedPreferences with AndroidX.Preference equivalent or Context.GetSharedPreferences with an explicit name — which approach, and does it preserve the existing default-preferences storage location/keys for already-installed apps?

Restricting DataContext (DataContextProperty, DataContextChanged, OnDataContextChanged) emission to FrameworkElement only is a broad generator change — what is the migration path for any DependencyObject-derived non-FrameworkElement type whose users currently set/observe DataContext?

Flipping project-level Nullable=enable is low runtime danger but surfaces many new warnings/annotations framework-wide. Should warnings be errors from the start, or staged? And is the per-file #nullable enable cleanup done in the same change?
Confirm no external binaries depend on the SetterBase.set_Property(string) shim before deleting it (binary-compat break).

Changing Duration.TimeSpan from a public mutable field to a read-only property: what do we do about the extra public Type field and public DurationType enum that WinUI does not expose — keep, hide, or remove?

Removing the Android-only public Window.IsStatusBarTranslucent() and redirecting ComboBox to the internal NativeWindowWrapper helper — confirm no external code calls the public method, or whether an [Obsolete] cycle is warranted.

Removing the throwing new setters on Count/IsReadOnly in ColorKeyFrameCollection: remove just the throwing setters, or the whole binary-compat new-shadow block? Confirm letting the get-only base members show through doesn't break existing collection-initializer XAML.

Removing Fluent V1 scaffolding (XamlControlsResourcesV1, ControlsResourcesVersion.Version1, the 4 FluentTheme.v1 projects) is a hard removal — confirm no shipping templates or consumer apps still request Version1 resources.

Moving Microsoft.Windows.ApplicationModel.Resources (MRT Core) out of Uno.UI into Uno.UWP or a dedicated assembly changes the type's shipping location — does this require an assembly-redirect/type-forward to avoid breaking compiled references, and which target assembly is preferred?

Removing/making-internal ~173 legacy Windows.UI.Input.* public types is a large surface removal. Should they be removed outright or hidden behind [EditorBrowsable(Never)]/[Obsolete] first, and is full migration to Microsoft.UI.Input.* available for every removed type?

synthesis.sequencingNotes:
Primary sort is danger (1 -> 5); within a tier I ordered by independence then effort (S before M before L). Excluded 7 items whose state is already-done (BC03, BC56, BC77, BC78) or obsolete (BC10, BC68, BC79) — they belong in no phase.

BATCH THESE: (a) Phase 1 native-only deletions ship as one PR — they share the 'native target dropped' justification and can't touch Skia. (b) The always-on/off feature-flag inlines (BC06, BC46, BC69, BC33, BC18, BC45 plus BC35) are mechanically identical 'delete flag, keep live branch' edits — review them together in Phase 3. (c) The Setter pipeline trio (BC37 -> BC44 -> BC63) must land adjacently and in that order. (d) Phase 4 visibility tweaks and the typo/signature fixes (BC62, BC41, BC28, BC64) are independent one-liners ideal for a single sweep.

GATES / WATCH: BC58 gates BC54 and de-risks BC26 (all touch the DataContext/DO generator emission) — do the generator-wide change first, then the FlyoutBase symptom. BC38 (Background -> Control) precedes BC14 (UserControl -> Control) so the property relocation isn't redone. BC37/BC44 must precede deleting Setter<T> consumers. The four danger-4/5 type-system items (BC58, BC54, BC26, BC14) should each land as their own separately-stabilized PR with full runtime-test passes, never batched.

BEHAVIORAL-DRIFT WATCH (Phase 5): BC50 (culture no longer changes on setter), BC45 (Center/Center default), BC74 (drawable key collision fix), BC42 (drop clr-namespace:), and BC51 (ms-resource:/// rewrite) silently change runtime behavior even for default users — gate each on a migration note and visual/runtime validation, not just compile.

POSSIBLY-DONE: BC07 (bootstrapper meta-packages) is flagged possibly-done; verify before doing work — if already removed, move it to doneOrObsolete. BC21 is 'unclear' (the targeted member is actually a valid public WinUI property) — resolve the decision question before touching AutomationProperties.cs; it may also drop out entirely like BC10.
